case_name: Salomon v. A Salomon and Co Ltd
legal_principle: A duly incorporated company is a separate legal entity distinct from its members. The corporate veil cannot be pierced merely because the company is a one-man company or because incorporation was used to limit liability. This is the foundational case on separate corporate personality.
key_quote: The company is at law a different person altogether from the subscribers to the memorandum.
full_summary: Salomon incorporated his boot-making business as a limited company with himself and six family members as shareholders. He held the vast majority of shares and was also a secured creditor. When the company became insolvent, the liquidator argued Salomon should be personally liable for the company's debts as the company was merely his agent or alias. The House of Lords unanimously reversed the Court of Appeal, holding that once a company is validly incorporated under the Companies Act, it is a separate legal person distinct from its members regardless of the motives for incorporation or the extent of one shareholder's control. The decision established the fundamental principle of separate corporate personality in company law, which has been applied consistently across common law jurisdictions including Ireland under the Companies Act 2014.

legal_principle: The corporate veil will only be pierced in limited circumstances: where the company is a mere facade or sham concealing the true facts, or where the subsidiary is authorised to act as agent for the parent. The court rejected broad single economic unit and justice-based approaches to veil-piercing.
key_quote: The court is not free to disregard the principle of Salomon merely because it considers that justice so requires.
full_summary: Cape Industries was an English company that mined asbestos in South Africa through a complex group structure including US subsidiaries. Workers injured by asbestos sought to enforce US judgments against Cape in England. The Court of Appeal conducted a comprehensive review of veil-piercing jurisprudence and held that the corporate veil would only be pierced in three limited situations: (1) where the court is interpreting a statute or contract; (2) where the company is a mere facade concealing the true facts; and (3) where the subsidiary was authorised to act as agent for the parent. The court rejected the single economic unit theory and the approach that the veil could be pierced simply in the interests of justice. This decision remains the leading English authority on the limits of veil-piercing and has been influential in Irish law.

legal_principle: The corporate veil will be pierced where a company is used as a device or sham to evade an existing legal obligation. Specific performance was ordered against both the individual and the company he had formed to avoid completing a contract for the sale of land.
key_quote: The defendant company is the creature of the first defendant, a device and a sham, a mask which he holds before his face in an attempt to avoid recognition by the eye of equity.
full_summary: Lipman contracted to sell land to Jones but then changed his mind. To evade the contract, he incorporated a company and transferred the land to it. Jones sought specific performance against both Lipman and the company. Russell J held that the company was a mere device or sham created to evade Lipman's existing contractual obligation. The court pierced the corporate veil and ordered specific performance against both the individual and the company. The case established the principle that the veil will be pierced where a company is used as an instrument to evade an existing legal obligation that the controller personally bears.

legal_principle: Irish authority confirming the Salomon principle. A company is a separate legal person from its members and directors. The corporate veil cannot be pierced simply because the company is controlled by one person or a small group.
key_quote: The separate legal personality of a company, even one controlled by a single individual, must be respected.
full_summary: This case involved a prosecution for safety offences on a building site. The question arose whether the directing mind of the company could be personally liable for the company's actions. The Supreme Court reaffirmed the principle from Salomon v. Salomon that a company is a separate legal person from its shareholders and directors. The court held that the mere fact that a company is controlled by one person does not justify disregarding the separate personality of the company. However, the court acknowledged that there are limited circumstances in which the veil may be pierced, including where the company is used as a device to evade legal obligations. This is an important Irish authority confirming the application of the Salomon principle in Irish company law.

case_name: Power Supermarkets Ltd v. Crumlin Investments Ltd
legal_principle: The court pierced the corporate veil where a group of related companies was used to defeat the contractual rights of a tenant. Where a company within a group acts as agent for the parent or other group members, the court may treat the group as a single entity for the purpose of enforcing rights.
key_quote: The court will not permit the corporate structure to be used as a mechanism to defeat the legitimate interests of third parties.
full_summary: Dunnes Stores had a lease restricting the landlord from letting to a competitor. The landlord's associated company let premises to a Quinnsworth subsidiary. Power Supermarkets argued the group structure should be disregarded. Costello J in the High Court held that the corporate veil could be pierced where the corporate structure was being used to defeat the contractual rights of a third party. The court treated the associated companies as a single entity for the purpose of the restrictive covenant. This is a leading Irish authority on veil-piercing in the context of group structures and has been widely cited in subsequent cases.

legal_principle: The UK Supreme Court distinguished between the concealment principle (where the veil is not truly pierced but the court identifies the true legal relationship) and the evasion principle (where the veil is pierced because the company is interposed to evade an existing obligation). Veil-piercing is only justified under the evasion principle.
key_quote: The corporate veil may be pierced only to prevent the abuse of corporate legal personality where a person is under an existing legal obligation which he deliberately evades by interposing a company.
full_summary: In matrimonial proceedings, the wife sought a share of properties held by companies controlled by her husband. The UK Supreme Court conducted a comprehensive review of veil-piercing jurisprudence. Lord Sumption distinguished between the concealment principle (where the court looks behind the corporate structure to identify the true legal relationship) and the evasion principle (where the veil is pierced because a company has been interposed to evade an existing legal obligation). The court held that veil-piercing is only truly justified under the evasion principle. While the corporate veil was not ultimately pierced in this case (the properties were transferred on other grounds), the decision provides the most authoritative modern analysis of when veil-piercing is available. The case is highly influential in Irish company law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on veil-piercing in the context of connected companies. The court will look behind the corporate structure where companies within a group are not truly independent and are used to facilitate a fraud or improper purpose.
key_quote: Where connected companies are not truly independent, the court may treat them as a single entity.
full_summary: This case involved related companies in the travel industry. The High Court examined the circumstances in which the separate personality of connected companies within a group could be disregarded. The court held that where companies within a group are not truly independent and the corporate structure is being used to facilitate an improper purpose, the court may treat the connected companies as a single entity. The decision is an early Irish authority on the application of veil-piercing principles to group structures and has been cited alongside Power Supermarkets in subsequent cases on connected companies.

case_name: VTB Capital plc v. Nutritek International Corporation
legal_principle: The UK Supreme Court confirmed that the corporate veil cannot be pierced simply because a company was used to structure a transaction that caused loss. The claimant must show that the company was a facade or that it was interposed to evade an existing obligation.
key_quote: Piercing the corporate veil is not available simply because a company has been used to structure a transaction.
full_summary: VTB Capital lent money on the basis of fraudulent misrepresentations. It sought to hold the controlling shareholder of the borrower personally liable by piercing the corporate veil. The UK Supreme Court refused to pierce the veil, holding that the mere fact that a company was used to structure a transaction that later turned out to be fraudulent did not justify veil-piercing. The court held that veil-piercing requires the company to have been used as a facade to conceal the true facts or to evade an existing legal obligation. The company in this case had genuine legal obligations and was not a sham. The decision confirmed the narrow scope of veil-piercing in English law.

legal_principle: The House of Lords held that the corporate veil will not be pierced merely because a group of companies operates as a single economic unit. The veil may only be lifted where there is a facade or sham.
key_quote: It is appropriate to pierce the corporate veil only where special circumstances exist indicating that it is a mere facade concealing the true facts.
full_summary: Woolfson and his wife owned shops through a company. When the premises were compulsorily acquired, Woolfson claimed compensation on the basis that he and the company were effectively the same. The House of Lords refused to pierce the veil, holding that the corporate structure was not a facade. Lord Keith stated that the veil should only be pierced where special circumstances exist indicating that the corporate structure is a mere facade concealing the true facts. The decision confirmed the narrow scope of veil-piercing and rejected the argument that a company controlled by one person is automatically a facade.

legal_principle: The court pierced the corporate veil where two shareholders formed a new company to make a compulsory acquisition offer for the third shareholder's shares, effectively using the corporate form to expropriate the minority shareholder.
key_quote: The court will not allow the device of forming a new company to be used to deprive a minority shareholder of the protection afforded by statute.
full_summary: Two shareholders holding 90% of the shares in Bugle Press formed a new company (Jackson and Shaw) to make a compulsory acquisition bid for the remaining 10% held by the third shareholder, Treby. The Court of Appeal pierced the veil of the new company, holding that it was a mere nominee or agent for the two majority shareholders and was formed solely for the purpose of expropriating the minority shareholder's shares. The court refused to allow the compulsory acquisition to proceed as the new company was not a genuine independent purchaser but a device created by the existing majority shareholders. The case illustrates the principle that the veil will be pierced where a company is used as an instrument to defeat statutory protections.

case_name: DHN Food Distributors Ltd v. Tower Hamlets LBC
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal treated a parent company and its wholly-owned subsidiaries as a single economic unit for the purpose of assessing compensation for compulsory acquisition. This decision has been criticised and its authority has been doubted.
key_quote: These three companies should, for present purposes, be treated as one.
full_summary: DHN carried on business through a group structure where one subsidiary owned the premises and another held the vehicles. When the premises were compulsorily acquired, the question was whether DHN could claim compensation for disturbance to its business. The Court of Appeal treated the three companies as a single economic unit, allowing DHN to claim compensation. Lord Denning MR held that the group should be treated as one entity. However, this decision has been heavily criticised and doubted by subsequent authorities including Woolfson and Adams v. Cape Industries. The single economic unit doctrine it proposed has not been accepted as a general principle.

legal_principle: The corporate veil was pierced where a company was a mere puppet or agent of another entity. A British company formed to qualify a film for registration as British was held to be a mere agent for the American production company that controlled the film's production.
key_quote: The company was a mere puppet, and the real maker of the film was the American corporation.
full_summary: FG Films Ltd was a British company that sought to register a film as British under the Cinematograph Films Act. The film had been produced with the resources and under the control of an American corporation. The High Court held that FG Films was merely acting as the nominee or agent of the American corporation and that the real maker of the film was the American company. The court pierced the corporate veil to look at the substance of the arrangement rather than its form. The case illustrates the agency basis for piercing the corporate veil, where the company in question is not acting independently but as a puppet or agent of another party.

case_name: Smith, Stone and Knight Ltd v. Birmingham Corporation
legal_principle: A subsidiary company may be treated as the agent of its parent company where the subsidiary carries on the parent's business rather than its own. The court established six tests for determining whether a subsidiary is acting as agent for its parent.
key_quote: The subsidiary was carrying on the business of the parent company as its agent.
full_summary: Birmingham Corporation compulsorily acquired premises occupied by a subsidiary of Smith, Stone and Knight. The parent company claimed compensation for disturbance on the basis that the subsidiary was carrying on the parent's business. Atkinson J held that the subsidiary was indeed acting as agent for the parent and the parent was entitled to compensation. The court established six tests for determining whether a subsidiary is carrying on the parent's business: (1) were the profits of the subsidiary treated as the profits of the parent; (2) were the persons conducting the business appointed by the parent; (3) was the parent the head and brain of the trading venture; (4) did the parent govern the adventure; (5) were the profits made by the parent's skill and direction; (6) was the parent in effectual and constant control. This case has been influential but the agency basis has been narrowed by later authorities.

case_name: State (McInerney and Co Ltd) v. Dublin County Council
legal_principle: Irish authority on the separate personality of companies within a group. The Supreme Court confirmed that each company within a group has its own separate legal personality and the assets of one company cannot be attributed to another simply because they are part of the same group.
key_quote: Each company within a group is a separate legal entity and the assets of one cannot be attributed to another merely on the basis of group membership.
full_summary: McInerney and Co Ltd challenged a planning decision by Dublin County Council. The question arose whether the resources and operations of related companies within the McInerney group could be attributed to the applicant company. The Supreme Court confirmed the Salomon principle that each company within a group has its own separate legal personality. The court held that the assets and liabilities of one company within a group cannot be attributed to another company simply because they are part of the same corporate group. This is a leading Irish authority on the application of separate corporate personality in the context of group structures.

case_name: Gilford Motor Co Ltd v. Horne
legal_principle: The corporate veil was pierced where a former employee formed a company to solicit customers in breach of a restraint of trade covenant. The company was held to be a mere cloak or sham used to evade the covenant.
key_quote: The company was formed as a device, a stratagem, to mask the effective carrying on of business by the defendant in breach of his covenant.
full_summary: Horne was a former managing director of Gilford Motor Co who was bound by a restraint of trade covenant. After leaving, he formed a company through which he solicited the former employer's customers. The Court of Appeal pierced the corporate veil, holding that the company was a mere cloak or sham formed as a device to enable Horne to breach his covenant while technically staying within its terms. An injunction was granted against both Horne personally and the company. The case established the principle that the corporate veil will be pierced where a company is used as a device to evade an existing personal legal obligation, and is frequently cited alongside Jones v. Lipman.

legal_principle: An outsider dealing with a company in good faith is entitled to assume that all internal procedures and formalities required by the company's constitution have been complied with. This is known as the indoor management rule or the Turquand rule.
key_quote: Persons dealing with a company are entitled to assume that all internal regulations have been duly complied with.
full_summary: The directors of a company borrowed money by issuing a bond. The company's deed of settlement required a resolution of the general meeting to authorise borrowing. No such resolution had been passed. The company sought to avoid liability on the bond. The court held that outsiders dealing with a company are entitled to assume that all internal procedures required by the company's constitution have been complied with. The bondholder was not required to inquire whether the internal resolution had been passed. This established the indoor management rule (Turquand rule), a fundamental principle of company law that protects third parties dealing with companies. The rule is now partially codified in s.39 of the Companies Act 2014 in Ireland.

case_name: Freeman and Lockyer v. Buckhurst Park Properties (Mangal) Ltd
legal_principle: A company may be bound by the acts of a person who has not been formally appointed as agent but who has been held out by the company as having authority to act on its behalf (ostensible or apparent authority). The four conditions for apparent authority were established.
key_quote: Ostensible authority is the authority of an agent as it appears to others.
full_summary: Kapoor acted as managing director of the company without being formally appointed. He engaged architects on the company's behalf. The company refused to pay, arguing Kapoor had no authority. The Court of Appeal held the company liable on the basis of ostensible or apparent authority. Diplock LJ set out four conditions for a company to be bound by apparent authority: (1) a representation was made that the agent had authority; (2) the representation was made by a person with actual authority to manage the company's business; (3) the third party relied on the representation; and (4) the company was not deprived of capacity to enter the transaction. This is the leading authority on apparent authority in company law and is essential reading for FE-1.

case_name: Mahony v. East Holyford Mining Co
legal_principle: A person dealing in good faith with a company through its officers is not bound to inquire into the internal arrangements of the company. The Turquand rule protects outsiders from irregularities in the internal management of the company.
key_quote: All that the directors did was done within the scope of the authority that a trading company could confer.
full_summary: The East Holyford Mining Company was formed with fictitious shareholders and directors. A bank dealt with the company through persons who purported to be directors and opened accounts. When the fraud was discovered, the question arose whether the bank's transactions were valid. The House of Lords held that the bank was entitled to assume that the persons purporting to be directors had been properly appointed and were acting within their authority. The bank was not required to inquire into the internal arrangements of the company. This case reinforced and extended the Turquand indoor management rule and is frequently cited as authority for the proposition that outsiders are protected from irregularities in a company's internal management.

case_name: Irvine v. Union Bank of Australia
legal_principle: A company is not bound by acts of its agent that are outside the scope of the agent's actual or apparent authority. A third party who deals with an agent knowing that the agent lacks authority cannot rely on the Turquand rule.
key_quote: Where a party has notice that the agent is exceeding his authority, the indoor management rule affords no protection.
full_summary: The Privy Council considered whether a bank could rely on the indoor management rule where the agent purporting to act on behalf of the company exceeded the scope of his authority. The court held that the Turquand rule does not protect a third party who has actual knowledge that the agent is exceeding his authority or where the circumstances are such as to put the third party on inquiry. The decision established an important limitation on the indoor management rule: it only protects parties dealing in good faith and without notice of the irregularity.

legal_principle: A chairman of the board who acts as de facto managing director may have implied actual authority to bind the company, even without formal appointment. The board's acquiescence in the chairman's conduct of business amounted to implied authority.
key_quote: Where the board acquiesces in the conduct of one of its members acting as managing director, that member has implied actual authority.
full_summary: Lord Suirdale was chairman of Brayhead Ltd and acted as de facto managing director with the board's knowledge and acquiescence. He gave personal guarantees and indemnities on behalf of the company without board authorisation. The Court of Appeal held that Lord Suirdale had implied actual authority to bind the company based on the board's acquiescence in his conduct of the company's affairs over time. Lord Denning MR distinguished between actual authority (express or implied) and apparent authority (represented to third parties). The case is an important authority on implied actual authority and the distinction between types of agent authority in company law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the application of the doctrine of ostensible authority. A company may be bound by the acts of a person who is held out as having authority to act on the company's behalf, even where the person lacks actual authority.
key_quote: Where a company holds out a person as having authority to act on its behalf, the company is bound by that person's acts.
full_summary: The plaintiff brought proceedings against a company and its agents on the basis that a person purporting to act on behalf of the company had entered into commitments. The High Court applied the principles of ostensible authority as established in Freeman and Lockyer v. Buckhurst Park Properties, holding that where a company holds out a person as having authority to act on its behalf, the company will be bound by the acts of that person vis-à-vis third parties who deal with the company in reliance on the representation. The case is an Irish application of the English authority on apparent authority and confirms the applicability of the four-part test from Freeman and Lockyer in Irish law.

case_name: Williamson v. Bailieborough Co-operative Agricultural and Dairy Society
legal_principle: Irish authority on ultra vires and corporate capacity. Where a company's objects clause is construed narrowly, acts outside the objects may be void and unenforceable against the company.
key_quote: A company's capacity to act is defined by its objects clause, and acts outside those objects are ultra vires and void.
full_summary: The case concerned a challenge to acts of a co-operative society on the basis that they were ultra vires the society's objects. The High Court examined the doctrine of ultra vires as applied to corporate bodies in Ireland, considering the scope of the objects clause and whether the impugned acts fell within the company's capacity. The judgment applied the established principles on the interpretation of objects clauses and the consequences of ultra vires acts. Under the Companies Act 2014, the ultra vires doctrine has been substantially reformed for companies registered under that Act, but the principles remain relevant for older entities and examination purposes.

case_name: Underwood Ltd v. Bank of Liverpool
legal_principle: A third party cannot rely on the indoor management rule where the circumstances are suspicious and should put a reasonable person on inquiry. The Turquand rule does not protect a party who is put on notice of irregularity.
key_quote: Where the circumstances are such as to put a reasonable person on inquiry, the indoor management rule does not apply.
full_summary: A director of Underwood Ltd endorsed cheques payable to the company and paid them into his own personal bank account. The company sued the bank for conversion. The Court of Appeal held that the bank could not rely on the indoor management rule because the circumstances (a director paying company cheques into his personal account) were suspicious and should have put the bank on inquiry. The case established an important exception to the Turquand rule: a third party who is put on notice of irregularity by suspicious circumstances cannot rely on the assumption that internal procedures have been complied with.

legal_principle: The agent himself cannot be the source of the representation of his own authority. Apparent authority requires the representation to come from the principal (the company), not from the agent who claims to have authority.
key_quote: No representation by the agent as to the extent of his authority can amount to a holding out by the principal.
full_summary: Magelssen, an officer of Mundogas, purported to enter into a charterparty on behalf of the company without authority. He represented to the third party that he had authority to do so. The House of Lords held that the company was not bound because the representation of authority must come from the principal (the company), not from the agent himself. An agent cannot unilaterally clothe himself with apparent authority by representing that he has authority. The case is a leading authority on the requirement that the representation in ostensible authority must emanate from the principal.

case_name: ESS Food AB v. Crown Shipping (Ireland) Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the distinction between actual and apparent authority in company law. The court applied the principles from Freeman and Lockyer to determine whether a company's employee had authority to bind the company.
key_quote: The test for apparent authority requires examination of the representations made by the company to the third party.
full_summary: ESS Food brought proceedings against Crown Shipping on the basis that an employee of Crown Shipping had entered into a contract on behalf of the company. The issue was whether the employee had actual or apparent authority to bind the company. The High Court applied the principles from Freeman and Lockyer v. Buckhurst Park Properties, examining whether the company had represented to ESS Food that the employee had authority to enter the contract. The court considered the employee's role, the company's conduct, and the third party's reliance. This is an Irish authority on the application of the apparent authority doctrine in the context of company employee transactions.

case_name: Ulster Factors Ltd v. Entonglen Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the indoor management rule. Third parties dealing with a company are entitled to assume that internal resolutions and procedures required by the company's constitution have been complied with.
key_quote: An outsider is entitled to presume that all matters of internal management have been duly carried out.
full_summary: Ulster Factors entered into a factoring arrangement with Entonglen Ltd. A dispute arose as to whether the company had properly authorised the arrangement through the required internal procedures. The High Court applied the Turquand indoor management rule, holding that the third party was entitled to assume that all internal procedures had been complied with, in the absence of notice to the contrary. The case is an Irish application of the Turquand rule and confirms its continued relevance in Irish company law, subject now to the statutory provisions of the Companies Act 2014.

case_name: United Bank of Kuwait v. Hammoud
legal_principle: A principal may be bound by the acts of an agent where the agent acts within the scope of his usual or customary authority, even if the agent's actual authority has been restricted. The test is whether a reasonable person would believe the agent had authority.
key_quote: A solicitor acting in a conveyancing transaction has usual authority to give an undertaking on behalf of his client.
full_summary: A solicitor gave undertakings on behalf of his client in a property transaction, but the undertakings were given for fraudulent purposes. The bank relied on the undertakings. The Court of Appeal held that the solicitor had usual authority to give such undertakings in the context of a conveyancing transaction, and the bank was entitled to rely on them. The court applied the doctrine of usual or customary authority, under which a third party is entitled to assume that an agent has the authority customarily associated with the position the agent holds, unless the third party has notice that the authority has been restricted.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the constructive notice doctrine and its interaction with the indoor management rule. A person dealing with a company is deemed to have constructive notice of the contents of documents filed in the Companies Registration Office.
key_quote: The doctrine of constructive notice means that third parties are deemed to know the contents of public filings.
full_summary: The case addressed the interaction between the constructive notice doctrine (under which third parties are deemed to know the contents of documents filed with the CRO) and the indoor management rule (under which third parties are entitled to assume internal procedures have been followed). The High Court examined the scope of each doctrine and the circumstances in which constructive notice might override the protection of the indoor management rule. The judgment is an Irish authority on the relationship between these two fundamental company law doctrines, both of which are now addressed by the Companies Act 2014.

case_name: Lennard's Carrying Co Ltd v. Asiatic Petroleum Co Ltd
legal_principle: A company acts through its directing mind and will. Where a person who is the directing mind and will of the company has the requisite knowledge or intention, that knowledge or intention is attributed to the company. This is the identification or alter ego doctrine.
key_quote: A corporation is an abstraction. It has no mind of its own any more than it has a body of its own; its active and directing will must consequently be sought in the person who is really the directing mind and will of the corporation.
full_summary: A cargo ship owned by Lennard's Carrying Co sank due to the unseaworthiness of the vessel. The question was whether the company had actual fault or privity. Viscount Haldane LC held that the fault of Lennard, who was the managing director and the person whose actions constituted the directing mind and will of the company, was the fault of the company itself. The case established the identification doctrine (also called the alter ego or directing mind doctrine), under which the knowledge, intention, and actions of the person who is the directing mind and will of the company are attributed to the company. This is a foundational principle of corporate attribution.

case_name: Firestone Tyre and Rubber Co v. Lewellin
legal_principle: A subsidiary company may be treated as the agent of its parent where the parent exercises such complete control over the subsidiary that the subsidiary is in substance the agent of the parent. The case concerned the tax liability of a subsidiary acting as agent.
key_quote: Where the parent exercises such a degree of control over the subsidiary that the subsidiary is in reality the agent of the parent, the parent may be held liable.
full_summary: The question was whether a UK subsidiary of a US parent company was carrying on the parent's business in the UK for tax purposes. The House of Lords held that the subsidiary was acting as agent for the parent and that the parent was therefore carrying on business in the UK through its subsidiary. The court examined the degree of control exercised by the parent over the subsidiary's operations and concluded that the subsidiary had no real independence. The case illustrates the agency basis for attributing the acts of a subsidiary to its parent and is cited in the context of group company relationships and tax liability.

case_name: Hickman v. Kent or Romney Marsh Sheepbreeders' Association
legal_principle: The articles of association constitute a contract between the company and its members, and between the members inter se, in respect of their rights as members. A member can enforce the articles against the company and other members in their capacity as members.
key_quote: The articles of association constitute a contract between the company and each member and between the members inter se.
full_summary: Hickman, a member of the association, challenged a decision to expel him. The articles required disputes between the company and members to be referred to arbitration. The court held that the articles constituted a contract between the company and its members, and between the members inter se, enforceable by any member in their capacity as member. Astbury J confirmed that this statutory contract (under what is now s.31 of the Companies Act 2014 in Ireland) binds the company and its members as if each had covenanted to observe its provisions. The case is the leading authority on the contractual nature of the articles of association.

case_name: Eley v. Positive Government Security Life Assurance Co
legal_principle: The articles of association do not create contractual rights in favour of a person in a capacity other than as member. An outsider (non-member) or a member in a non-member capacity cannot enforce the articles.
key_quote: The articles do not constitute a contract between the company and a person in a capacity other than as member.
full_summary: Eley was named in the company's articles as the company's solicitor for life. He was also a shareholder. When the company ceased to employ him, he sued on the articles. The Court of Appeal held that the articles did not create enforceable contractual rights in favour of Eley in his capacity as solicitor (an outsider right). The statutory contract created by the articles only operates between the company and its members in their capacity as members. Eley could not enforce the articles to secure his position as solicitor because that was an outsider right, not a membership right.

legal_principle: The articles create a contract between the members inter se (not just between the company and its members). A member can enforce the articles directly against other members where the obligation relates to membership rights.
key_quote: The articles constitute a contract not merely between the shareholders and the company but between the shareholders inter se.
full_summary: The articles of the company provided that any member wishing to transfer shares must inform the directors who were obliged to purchase them at a fair price. The plaintiff member wished to sell his shares and the directors (who were also members) refused to purchase. Vaisey J held that the articles created a contract between the members inter se, and the obligation to purchase fell on the directors in their capacity as members (since all directors were required to be members). The case is important authority for the proposition that the statutory contract created by the articles operates not only between the company and its members but also between the members themselves.

case_name: Bailey v. New South Wales Medical Defence Union Ltd
court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
legal_principle: A member may enforce the articles of association against the company where the right sought to be enforced is a membership right (a right given to the member in their capacity as member) as opposed to an outsider right.
key_quote: The distinction between insider rights and outsider rights determines which provisions of the articles are enforceable by a member.
full_summary: The plaintiff, a member of the medical defence union, sought to enforce provisions of the articles that related to the provision of legal assistance to members. The court examined the distinction between insider rights (rights given to members in their capacity as members, which are enforceable) and outsider rights (rights given to persons in some other capacity, which are not enforceable under the statutory contract). The judgment analysed the scope of the statutory contract created by the articles and the categories of rights that are properly characterised as membership rights. The case provides useful guidance on the classification of rights as insider or outsider rights.

case_name: Lee v. Lee's Air Farming Ltd
legal_principle: A person can be both the controlling shareholder and an employee of a company. The Salomon principle of separate legal personality means that a director and sole shareholder can enter into a valid contract of employment with the company he controls.
key_quote: It is well established that the mere fact that a person is a director of a company is no impediment to his entering into a contract to serve the company.
full_summary: Mr Lee was the sole governing director and majority shareholder of Lee's Air Farming Ltd. He was killed in a flying accident while working for the company. His widow claimed workers' compensation. The company's insurer argued that Lee could not be both employer and employee. The Privy Council held that the company had a separate legal personality from Lee and that Lee was validly employed by the company under a contract of service. The Salomon principle meant that the company and Lee were separate legal persons capable of contracting with each other. The case is essential authority on the ability of directors and shareholders to be employees of their own company.

case_name: McMahon v. Irish Road Haulage Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of articles of association. The articles create binding obligations between the company and its members that are enforceable through the courts.
key_quote: The articles of association are enforceable as a contract between the company and its members.
full_summary: McMahon, a member of Irish Road Haulage Ltd, sought to enforce provisions of the company's articles of association against the company. The High Court confirmed that the articles constitute a contract between the company and its members, enforceable through the courts. The judgment applied the principles from Hickman v. Kent regarding the statutory contract nature of the articles and confirmed their application in Irish law. The case is an Irish authority on the contractual effect of the articles under company law and is regularly cited in FE-1 company law materials.

case_name: Bratton Seymour Service Co Ltd v. Oxborough
legal_principle: The statutory contract created by the articles of association cannot be supplemented by implied terms derived from the parties' subjective expectations. Only the express terms of the articles constitute the statutory contract.
key_quote: The statutory contract must be construed as it stands and cannot be supplemented by terms derived from the parties' expectations.
full_summary: The case concerned a management company for a block of flats. Members argued that terms should be implied into the articles based on the parties' expectations at the time of purchase. The Court of Appeal rejected this argument, holding that the statutory contract created by the articles is a contract of a special nature. It cannot be supplemented by implied terms derived from the parties' subjective expectations or commercial considerations. The articles must be construed as they stand, and only their express terms constitute the statutory contract. The case is important for understanding the special nature of the statutory contract and its limitations.

legal_principle: A member suing in an individual capacity can only enforce rights conferred on him as a member by the articles. The articles do not confer enforceable rights on persons in a non-member capacity, such as a director or promoter.
key_quote: A member cannot enforce the articles in respect of rights that accrue to him in any capacity other than as member.
full_summary: Browne was both a shareholder and a director of La Trinidad. He sought to enforce provisions of the articles that related to his position as director. The Court of Appeal held that the articles did not confer enforceable rights on Browne in his capacity as director. The statutory contract created by the articles operates only in respect of membership rights. Rights that accrue to a person in their capacity as director, promoter, solicitor, or any other non-member capacity are outsider rights and cannot be enforced under the statutory contract. The case reinforces the insider/outsider distinction established in Eley v. Positive Government.

case_name: Macaura v. Northern Assurance Co Ltd
legal_principle: A shareholder has no insurable interest in the assets of the company. The company's assets belong to the company, not to the shareholders, as a consequence of the principle of separate legal personality.
key_quote: No shareholder has any right to any item of property owned by the company, for he has no legal or equitable interest therein.
full_summary: Macaura was the sole beneficial owner of a timber estate which he transferred to a company in exchange for shares. He insured the timber in his own name. When the timber was destroyed by fire, the insurance company refused to pay, arguing Macaura had no insurable interest. The House of Lords held that as a shareholder, Macaura had no insurable interest in the company's property. The assets belonged to the company, a separate legal person, and not to its shareholders. A shareholder's interest is in the shares themselves, not in the underlying assets of the company. This case is a fundamental illustration of the consequences of separate legal personality.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforceability of the articles of association. A member who is also a director may enforce provisions of the articles that relate to membership rights, but not provisions that relate solely to his position as director.
key_quote: The articles may confer rights enforceable by a member, but only insofar as those rights relate to his capacity as member.
full_summary: Glover was a director and shareholder of BLN Ltd. He was removed from his position as director and sought to enforce provisions of the articles. The High Court examined the distinction between rights arising from membership and rights arising from directorship. The court held that while Glover could enforce provisions of the articles that related to his membership rights, he could not enforce provisions that related solely to his position as director, as these were outsider rights. The judgment is an important Irish authority on the insider/outsider distinction in the context of the statutory contract created by the articles.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the interpretation of articles of association in the context of co-operative societies. The articles must be construed in accordance with their natural and ordinary meaning, having regard to the objects and purposes of the company.
key_quote: The articles of association must be construed in accordance with their natural and ordinary meaning.
full_summary: The case concerned the interpretation of the articles of association of a co-operative creamery. The High Court examined the principles of construction applicable to articles of association, holding that the articles must be interpreted in accordance with their natural and ordinary meaning, read in the context of the articles as a whole and having regard to the objects and purposes of the company. The judgment addressed the particular features of co-operative societies and the application of company law principles to such entities. The case is cited as an Irish authority on the interpretation of articles.

case_name: Port Darwin Gold Mining Co, Re
legal_principle: The articles of association bind every member as if they had personally executed a deed containing the articles. A member is bound by the articles from the moment of becoming a member, even if they have not read them.
key_quote: Every member is bound by the articles as if he had personally executed them.
full_summary: The case concerned the enforcement of the articles of association against a member. The Court of Appeal confirmed that the articles bind every member as if they had personally executed a deed containing the articles, applying the principle of the statutory contract. A member is bound by all the provisions of the articles from the moment of becoming a member, whether or not they have actually read or understood them. The case reinforces the binding nature of the statutory contract and its application to all members without exception.

legal_principle: The Duomatic principle: where all shareholders with a right to attend and vote at a general meeting assent to a matter, that informal assent is as binding as a formal resolution. Also known as the unanimous consent principle.
key_quote: Where all shareholders assent to a matter, their informal assent is as effective as a resolution passed at a duly convened meeting.
full_summary: The case raised the question whether the informal assent of all shareholders could be treated as equivalent to a formal resolution of the company in general meeting. The High Court applied what would later become known as the Duomatic principle, holding that where all shareholders with a right to attend and vote at a general meeting assent to a particular course of action, that informal assent is as binding as a formal resolution properly passed at a general meeting. This principle avoids technical invalidity where the substance of shareholder approval exists but the procedural formalities were not followed. The case is an early Irish application of the unanimous consent principle.

legal_principle: Where all shareholders with a right to attend and vote at a general meeting assent to a matter which could have been effected by a resolution, that assent is as binding as a resolution. This is the Duomatic principle or unanimous consent rule.
key_quote: Where it can be shown that all shareholders who have a right to attend and vote at a general meeting of the company assent to some matter, that assent is as binding as a resolution.
full_summary: In the liquidation of Duomatic Ltd, the liquidator challenged payments made to directors as remuneration without formal shareholder approval by resolution. Buckley J held that the informal assent of all shareholders was sufficient. Where all shareholders who have a right to attend and vote at a general meeting assent to a matter which could have been effected by a resolution at a general meeting, that assent is as binding as a resolution in due form. This established the Duomatic principle (also called the unanimous consent principle), which is a fundamental doctrine of company law allowing informal shareholder approval to substitute for formal resolutions.

legal_principle: The Duomatic principle does not apply where a shareholder is unaware of the relevant facts or where the shareholders' consent is not truly unanimous. All shareholders entitled to vote must consent for the principle to operate.
key_quote: The Duomatic principle requires the consent of all shareholders entitled to vote, and that consent must be given with full knowledge of the relevant facts.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the limits of the Duomatic principle. The case involved a decision taken without the knowledge or consent of all shareholders. The court held that the Duomatic principle requires the genuine consent of all shareholders who are entitled to attend and vote at a general meeting. If any shareholder entitled to vote does not consent, or if shareholders consent without knowledge of the material facts, the principle does not apply and the decision is not binding. The case establishes important limitations on the Duomatic principle and is regularly cited alongside Re Duomatic.

case_name: Howard Smith Ltd v. Ampol Petroleum Ltd
legal_principle: Directors must exercise their powers for a proper purpose. An allotment of shares made primarily for the purpose of manipulating voting power rather than raising capital is an improper exercise of the power to allot and is voidable. The proper purpose doctrine applies regardless of the directors' good faith.
key_quote: It is not sufficient that the directors act in good faith; they must also exercise their powers for the purposes for which they were conferred.
full_summary: The directors of Millers allotted shares to Howard Smith to dilute the shareholding of Ampol, which was attempting a takeover. The directors believed the Howard Smith offer was better for the company but the primary purpose of the allotment was to shift the balance of voting power. The Privy Council held that the allotment was invalid because the power to allot shares was exercised for an improper purpose (manipulating voting control) rather than its proper purpose (raising capital). Lord Wilberforce held that even where directors act in good faith, the exercise of fiduciary powers for an improper purpose is voidable. The case establishes the proper purpose doctrine and is a leading authority on directors' duties under company law.

legal_principle: Directors must exercise their discretion bona fide in what they consider to be in the best interests of the company, and not for any collateral purpose. The court will not substitute its own judgment for that of the directors.
key_quote: Directors must exercise their discretion bona fide in what they consider — not what a court may consider — is in the interests of the company.
full_summary: The articles gave directors an absolute discretion to refuse to register a transfer of shares. Smith died and his executors sought registration of his shares. The directors refused to register the transfer. The Court of Appeal held that directors must exercise their discretion bona fide in what they consider to be in the best interests of the company, and not for any collateral purpose. The court will not interfere with the directors' exercise of discretion provided it is exercised in good faith and for a proper purpose. However, the burden is on the directors to show that their refusal was bona fide in the company's interest. This is a leading authority on the subjective nature of the duty of good faith.

legal_principle: Irish authority on directors' fiduciary duties. Directors must exercise their powers for the benefit of the company as a whole and not for their personal advantage. A director who exercises a power of veto over share transfers for personal reasons acts in breach of fiduciary duty.
key_quote: The power to refuse registration must be exercised for the benefit of the company as a whole and not for the personal advantage of the directors.
full_summary: The chairman of a company used his casting vote and his power under the articles to refuse to register a transfer of shares to a person he personally objected to. Ross J in the Irish Chancery Division held that the chairman had exercised his power for his personal advantage rather than for the benefit of the company as a whole. The court held that all fiduciary powers vested in directors must be exercised bona fide for the benefit of the company, and a director who exercises such powers for personal reasons is in breach of his fiduciary duty. This is a leading Irish authority on fiduciary duties of directors and the proper exercise of powers under the articles.

legal_principle: Directors who divert a corporate opportunity to themselves are in breach of their fiduciary duty to the company. The company is entitled to the benefit of the diverted opportunity, and a resolution of the shareholders purporting to ratify the breach is ineffective where the wrongdoing directors control the votes.
key_quote: Directors who have obtained for themselves a contract which it was their duty to obtain for the company cannot retain the benefit for themselves.
full_summary: Three of four directors of a construction company diverted a lucrative railway construction contract to themselves personally, excluding the fourth director. They then purported to have the company pass a resolution ratifying their actions. The Privy Council held that the directors were in breach of their fiduciary duty by diverting a corporate opportunity to themselves. The contract and its profits belonged in equity to the company. The purported ratification by shareholders' resolution was ineffective because the wrongdoing directors used their majority voting power to approve their own breach of duty. The case is a leading authority on the corporate opportunity doctrine and the limits of ratification.

case_name: Industrial Development Consultants Ltd v. Cooley
legal_principle: A director who obtains a corporate opportunity for himself while still a director is liable to account to the company for the profits, even if the company itself could not have obtained the contract. The no-profit rule is strictly applied.
key_quote: A director must account to the company for profits obtained from an opportunity that came to him by virtue of his position as director.
full_summary: Cooley was the managing director of IDC, an architectural consultancy. He learned that the Eastern Gas Board wanted to engage him personally for a project but would not deal with IDC. Cooley obtained his release from IDC on false pretences and then took the contract personally. Roskill J held that Cooley was liable to account to the company for all profits from the contract. The fiduciary no-profit rule was strictly applied: a director cannot retain for himself a profit arising from an opportunity that came to him by virtue of his position as director, regardless of whether the company could have obtained the contract itself. The case is a strict application of the corporate opportunity doctrine.

legal_principle: The test for whether directors have acted in good faith in the interests of the company is subjective: did the directors honestly believe that their actions were in the company's interests? The court will not substitute its own view of what was in the company's interests.
key_quote: The question is whether the director honestly believed that his act or omission was in the interests of the company. The issue is as to the director's state of mind.
full_summary: The liquidator of Regentcrest challenged a transaction entered into by the directors, alleging breach of the duty to act in good faith in the interests of the company. Jonathan Parker J held that the test for the duty of good faith is subjective: the question is whether the director honestly believed that his act or omission was in the interests of the company. It is not for the court to substitute its own view of what was in the company's interests. However, the court may draw an inference that a director did not honestly believe the action was in the company's interest if no intelligent and honest person could have so believed. The case is the leading authority on the subjective nature of the good faith duty.

legal_principle: Leading Irish authority on insider dealing and directors' duties in the context of share dealing by directors who possess price-sensitive information. The case examined the duty of directors not to use confidential company information for personal profit.
key_quote: A director who deals in the company's shares while in possession of price-sensitive information is in breach of his fiduciary duty to the company.
full_summary: Fyffes brought proceedings against DCC and its chief executive Jim Flavin alleging insider dealing in connection with the sale of Fyffes shares while in possession of price-sensitive information obtained through his position as a director. Laffoy J in the High Court conducted a detailed examination of the duties of directors in relation to confidential information and the prohibition on insider dealing under both common law fiduciary principles and statutory provisions. The case examined the meaning of price-sensitive information, the test for whether a reasonable investor would consider information material, and the obligations of directors who are also directors of other listed companies. This is the most significant Irish authority on insider dealing and directors' duties in the context of securities transactions.

case_name: Re City Equitable Fire Insurance Co Ltd
legal_principle: Directors are required to exercise the degree of skill and care that may reasonably be expected of a person of their knowledge and experience. The standard of care was historically subjective, taking into account the individual director's abilities.
key_quote: A director need not exhibit in the performance of his duties a greater degree of skill than may reasonably be expected from a person of his knowledge and experience.
full_summary: The company's chairman, Bevan, had been fraudulent and the company collapsed. The liquidator sought to hold the other directors liable for negligence. Romer J set out the historical principles on the duty of care owed by directors: (1) a director need not exhibit a greater degree of skill than may reasonably be expected from a person of his knowledge and experience; (2) a director is not bound to give continuous attention to the affairs of the company; and (3) a director may delegate duties to other officials and is entitled to trust them unless there are grounds for suspicion. While these principles have been substantially modified by modern statutory provisions (including s.228 of the Companies Act 2014), the case remains historically significant and is regularly cited in FE-1 examinations.

case_name: Boston Deep Sea Fishing and Ice Co v. Ansell
legal_principle: A director who receives a secret profit or commission in connection with the company's business is in breach of fiduciary duty and must account to the company for the profit. The company may also dismiss the director summarily.
key_quote: A director who receives a secret profit from a third party in connection with his position is liable to account to the company for that profit.
full_summary: Ansell was a director who received secret commissions from suppliers to the company and also made a personal profit from shares allocated to him as director. The Court of Appeal held that Ansell was in breach of his fiduciary duty to the company by receiving secret profits. He was liable to account to the company for all profits received. The court also held that the receipt of secret profits constituted grounds for summary dismissal, even though the company had not known of the profits at the time of dismissal. The case is a leading authority on the fiduciary no-profit rule and the strict liability of directors who receive secret commissions or profits.

case_name: Imperial Mercantile Credit Association v. Coleman
legal_principle: A director with a personal interest in a transaction with the company must disclose that interest. Failure to disclose a personal interest is a breach of fiduciary duty, and the transaction may be voidable at the company's option.
key_quote: A director who has a personal interest in a transaction with the company must make full disclosure of that interest.
full_summary: Coleman was a director who had a personal financial interest in a transaction entered into by the company. He failed to disclose this interest to the board. The House of Lords held that a director who has a personal interest in a transaction with the company is under a fiduciary obligation to make full disclosure of that interest to the board. Failure to do so is a breach of fiduciary duty and the transaction may be voidable at the company's option. The case established the foundational principle of the duty to disclose conflicts of interest, which is now codified in s.231 of the Companies Act 2014.

legal_principle: Where directors have a personal interest in a contract with the company but the articles permit such contracts subject to disclosure, the contract is valid provided the director has complied with the disclosure requirements in the articles.
key_quote: Where the articles permit a director to be interested in a contract with the company, the director must comply strictly with the disclosure requirements.
full_summary: The case concerned directors who had personal interests in contracts with the company. The articles of association permitted directors to be interested in contracts with the company subject to disclosure requirements. The Privy Council held that where the articles permit directors to contract with the company, the contracts are valid provided the directors comply with the disclosure requirements in the articles. The case illustrates the interaction between the common law duty to avoid conflicts of interest and the articles' provisions permitting directors' interests subject to disclosure.

legal_principle: A director who sells property to the company at a profit must disclose his interest in the transaction. The no-profit and no-conflict rules apply to transactions between a director and the company.
key_quote: A director who sells property to the company must make full and frank disclosure of his interest.
full_summary: Brookes was a promoter and later became a director of the company. He sold mining property to the company at a significant profit. The Court of Appeal held that a director who sells property to the company is in a fiduciary position and must make full disclosure of his interest in the transaction. The court applied the no-conflict and no-profit rules, holding that where a director profits from a transaction with the company, the transaction is voidable unless full disclosure has been made and the company has given its informed consent. The case illustrates the strict application of fiduciary principles to director-company transactions.

legal_principle: Directors have a duty to consider the interests of creditors when the company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency. The creditor duty modifies the general duty to act in the best interests of the company but does not create a direct duty owed to creditors.
key_quote: When a company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency, the interests of the company's creditors become a relevant consideration for directors.
full_summary: The UK Supreme Court considered the circumstances in which directors must consider the interests of creditors when exercising their fiduciary duties. Sequana had caused its subsidiary to pay a dividend when the subsidiary had a significant contingent liability. The Supreme Court held that directors have a duty to consider the interests of creditors when the company is insolvent or bordering on insolvency, or where an insolvent liquidation is probable. The duty does not create a direct cause of action for creditors but modifies the duty to act in the best interests of the company. The court identified three stages: (1) when the company is solvent, directors owe duties to shareholders; (2) when the company is in the zone of insolvency, directors must consider creditor interests; (3) when the company is insolvent, creditor interests become paramount. This is the most authoritative modern analysis of the creditor duty.

case_name: Charterhouse Investment Trust Ltd v. Tempest Diesels Ltd
legal_principle: Directors must act in the interests of the company, which means the interests of present and future shareholders. Where the company is solvent, directors' primary duty is to shareholders, not creditors.
key_quote: The duty of directors is to act in the interests of the company, meaning the shareholders as a general body.
full_summary: The case addressed the question of whose interests directors must consider when exercising their duties. Hoffmann J held that when directors act in the interests of the company, this means the interests of present and future shareholders as a general body, not the interests of any particular shareholder, employee, or creditor. When the company is solvent, the shareholders' interests are paramount. The judgment acknowledged that the position changes as the company approaches insolvency, when creditor interests become relevant. The case is an important authority on the meaning of acting in the interests of the company and is frequently cited alongside cases on the creditor duty.

case_name: Selanger United Rubber Estates Ltd v. Craddock (No 3)
legal_principle: A company's funds may not be used to finance the purchase of its own shares. A director who uses the company's money to finance a purchase of shares is in breach of fiduciary duty and any knowing recipient of the funds may be liable as constructive trustee.
key_quote: A director who causes the company's money to be applied for an improper purpose is in breach of his fiduciary duty.
full_summary: The company's funds were used to finance the acquisition of its own shares in breach of the prohibition on financial assistance. Ungoed-Thomas J held that the directors who authorised the use of company funds were in breach of their fiduciary duty, as the funds were applied for an improper purpose. Additionally, third parties who received the funds with knowledge that they were being misapplied were liable as constructive trustees. The case illustrates the intersection between the prohibition on financial assistance for the purchase of a company's own shares and directors' fiduciary duties.

legal_principle: The primary purpose of disqualification is the protection of the public, not punishment of the director. The court considers whether the director's conduct makes him unfit to be concerned in the management of a company.
key_quote: The primary purpose of disqualification is not to punish the individual but to protect the public against the future conduct of companies by persons whose past records show them to be unfit.
full_summary: The court considered the purpose and principles governing the disqualification of directors. Browne-Wilkinson V-C held that the primary purpose of disqualification orders is the protection of the public from persons whose past conduct has shown them to be unfit to be concerned in the management of a company. The court set out the factors to be considered, including the nature and extent of the director's misconduct, the number of companies involved, and the director's personal responsibility. The judgment established that disqualification is protective rather than punitive and remains a leading authority on the principles governing disqualification proceedings.

case_name: Re Tralee Beef and Lamb Ltd
legal_principle: Leading Irish authority on the restriction of directors under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014 (formerly s.150 of the 1990 Act). Directors of insolvent companies face restriction unless they can demonstrate they acted honestly and responsibly.
key_quote: The onus is on the director to satisfy the court that he has acted honestly and responsibly in relation to the conduct of the affairs of the company.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of the directors of an insolvent company under what is now s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court set out the principles governing restriction applications, holding that the burden is on the director to satisfy the court on the balance of probabilities that he acted honestly and responsibly in relation to the conduct of the company's affairs. The court identified the factors relevant to the assessment of responsible conduct, including compliance with statutory obligations, keeping proper books and records, making tax returns, and not engaging in reckless or fraudulent trading. This is the leading Irish authority on directors' restriction and is essential reading for FE-1.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court clarified the test for restriction under s.150 (now s.819). A director must demonstrate both honesty and responsibility. The test for responsibility requires the director to show that he exercised the standard of care reasonably to be expected.
key_quote: The requirement of acting responsibly demands a standard of care that a reasonable director would have exercised in the circumstances.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the interpretation and application of what is now s.819 of the Companies Act 2014 (formerly s.150 of the 1990 Act) in the context of an application to restrict directors of an insolvent company. The court clarified that the director must establish both honesty and responsibility. Honesty relates to the absence of fraud or dishonesty. Responsibility requires the director to demonstrate that he exercised the standard of care that could reasonably be expected of a person in his position. The court held that the standard is not that of the most diligent director but rather that of a reasonably competent director in the circumstances. This Supreme Court decision is the leading authority on the interpretation of the restriction provisions.

case_name: La Moselle Clothing Ltd v. Soualhi
legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court examined the factors relevant to whether a director has acted honestly and responsibly, including compliance with filing obligations, keeping proper books and records, and making appropriate tax returns.
key_quote: Failure to keep proper books and records and to make appropriate tax returns may indicate that a director has not acted responsibly.
full_summary: The liquidator applied to restrict the directors of La Moselle Clothing Ltd on the grounds that they had not acted honestly and responsibly. Shanley J in the High Court examined the factors relevant to the assessment of responsible conduct, including: whether the company kept proper books of account; whether annual returns were filed; whether tax returns were made; whether the directors took reasonable steps to ensure compliance with the Companies Acts; and whether the directors co-operated with the liquidator. The judgment is an important early Irish authority on the practical application of the restriction provisions and the factors the court will consider in determining whether directors have met the honest and responsible threshold.

legal_principle: The court examined the application of restriction provisions to directors of a large company that collapsed. Directors must demonstrate responsibility in the context of the size and complexity of the company's operations.
key_quote: The standard of responsibility expected of directors must be assessed in the context of the size and complexity of the company.
full_summary: USIT World plc was a major Irish travel company that collapsed, leaving significant debts. The liquidator applied for the restriction of the directors. Peart J in the High Court examined the conduct of each director against the statutory test of honesty and responsibility. The court considered the particular responsibilities of directors of a large public company, including oversight of financial controls, monitoring of the company's financial position, and ensuring compliance with statutory obligations. The judgment illustrates the application of the restriction test to directors of larger companies and the higher standard of responsibility expected in such contexts.

legal_principle: Directors have a duty to acquire and maintain a sufficient knowledge and understanding of the company's business to enable them to discharge their duties. A director who fails to monitor and supervise may be unfit to be a director.
key_quote: Directors have a continuing duty to acquire and maintain a sufficient knowledge and understanding of the company's business to enable them properly to discharge their duties.
full_summary: The case arose from the collapse of Barings Bank following the unauthorised trading activities of Nick Leeson. Jonathan Parker J examined the duties of the directors who had failed to supervise and monitor the bank's trading activities. The court held that directors have a continuing duty to acquire and maintain a sufficient knowledge and understanding of the company's business to enable them to properly discharge their duties as directors. A director who delegates responsibilities to others retains a duty to supervise and monitor the exercise of those delegated functions. The failure to implement adequate internal controls and risk management systems contributed to findings of unfitness. The case is a landmark authority on the duty of supervision and the objective standard of care expected of directors.

case_name: Re Colm O'Neill Engineering Services Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors where the company has failed to keep adequate books and records. The failure to maintain proper accounting records is a significant factor in assessing whether a director has acted responsibly.
key_quote: The failure to keep proper books and records is a factor that weighs heavily against a director in restriction proceedings.
full_summary: The liquidator applied to restrict the directors on the grounds that proper books and records had not been maintained. The High Court considered the statutory obligation to keep proper books of account and the consequences of failure to do so in the context of restriction proceedings. The court held that the failure to maintain adequate books and records is a significant factor weighing against a director seeking to demonstrate that he acted responsibly. The judgment illustrates the importance of compliance with book-keeping obligations in the context of directors' restriction.

legal_principle: Directors may be relieved from restriction where they can demonstrate that despite the company's insolvency, they acted honestly and took all reasonable steps to manage the company's affairs responsibly.
key_quote: A director may be relieved from restriction where he can show that he took all reasonable steps in the conduct of the company's affairs.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of the directors of an insolvent company. The directors sought to resist restriction by demonstrating that they had acted honestly and responsibly throughout the company's life. The High Court examined the specific steps taken by the directors to manage the company, including efforts to address financial difficulties, compliance with statutory obligations, and co-operation with the liquidator. The court assessed whether the directors had taken all reasonable steps that a responsible director would have taken in the circumstances. The case illustrates the circumstances in which directors may successfully resist restriction.

case_name: Re James Murphy and Sons Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the factors considered in restriction proceedings. The court examines the totality of the directors' conduct, not individual failings in isolation, to determine whether the honest and responsible threshold is met.
key_quote: The court must examine the totality of the director's conduct to determine whether he has acted honestly and responsibly.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of the directors of the insolvent company. The High Court examined the totality of the directors' conduct, including their management of the company's finances, their compliance with statutory obligations, and their dealings with creditors. The court held that the assessment of responsible conduct must be holistic, looking at the overall pattern of the directors' behaviour rather than focusing on individual failings in isolation. The judgment illustrates the practical application of the restriction test to a family-run business.

legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on the restriction of directors under the Companies Act 2014. The court applied the statutory factors under s.819 to assess the directors' conduct.
key_quote: The court must consider all the circumstances of the case in determining whether the director acted honestly and responsibly.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court applied the statutory criteria for assessing whether directors had acted honestly and responsibly. The court examined compliance with statutory obligations, maintenance of proper books of account, cooperation with the liquidator, and the general conduct of the directors in the management of the company's affairs. The judgment provides a modern application of the restriction provisions under the 2014 Act and illustrates the factors the court will consider.

legal_principle: The Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement may bring proceedings for the disqualification of directors. The court considers the nature and extent of the director's misconduct and the need to protect the public.
key_quote: Disqualification serves the public interest by removing from the management of companies persons whose conduct has demonstrated their unfitness.
full_summary: The ODCE brought proceedings for the disqualification of directors on the grounds of misconduct in the management of companies. The High Court examined the statutory framework for disqualification under what is now Part 14 of the Companies Act 2014. The court considered the nature and extent of the directors' misconduct, including failures to comply with statutory obligations, fraudulent conduct, and persistent default. The judgment addressed the purpose of disqualification (public protection rather than punishment) and the factors relevant to the length of the disqualification period.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors of an insolvent motor dealership. The court examined whether the directors had managed the company's trading and financial affairs with the degree of responsibility expected.
