key_quote: Directors who continue to trade when they know or ought to know that the company cannot meet its debts may not have acted responsibly.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Capital Auto Group. The High Court examined the directors' conduct in the period leading up to insolvency, particularly their decisions to continue trading when the company was in financial difficulty. The court considered whether the directors had taken appropriate steps to address the company's financial problems, had sought professional advice, and had considered the interests of creditors. The judgment illustrates the principle that directors who continue to trade without reasonable prospect of meeting the company's debts may be found not to have acted responsibly.

legal_principle: Directors must ensure that the company files annual returns and maintains proper books of account. Persistent failure to do so weighs against a finding of responsible conduct.
key_quote: Persistent failure to file annual returns demonstrates a disregard for statutory obligations that is inconsistent with responsible conduct.
full_summary: The liquidator applied to restrict the directors on the basis that the company had persistently failed to file annual returns and had not maintained proper books of account. The High Court held that the persistent failure to comply with basic statutory filing obligations weighed heavily against the directors in the assessment of whether they had acted responsibly. The court noted that the obligation to file annual returns and maintain books of account is a fundamental statutory duty that serves to protect creditors and the public. The judgment reinforces the importance of compliance with basic company law obligations in restriction proceedings.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the distinction between restriction and disqualification. Restriction is the default consequence for directors of insolvent companies unless the honest and responsible test is met, while disqualification requires a higher threshold of misconduct.
key_quote: Restriction and disqualification serve different purposes and have different thresholds.
full_summary: The case addressed the distinction between restriction and disqualification of directors in Irish company law. The High Court examined the different purposes and thresholds of each regime. Restriction (under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014) is the default consequence for directors of insolvent companies unless they can demonstrate they acted honestly and responsibly. Disqualification requires evidence of more serious misconduct, such as fraud, persistent breach of the Companies Acts, or unfitness. The judgment provided guidance on the circumstances in which each remedy is appropriate and the factors the court considers.

case_name: Secretary of State for Business v. Selby
legal_principle: Modern English authority on the disqualification of directors. The court considered the factors relevant to unfitness and the appropriate period of disqualification, including the degree of misconduct and the director's personal culpability.
key_quote: The period of disqualification must reflect both the gravity of the misconduct and the need to protect the public.
full_summary: The Secretary of State applied for the disqualification of directors on the grounds of unfitness. The High Court examined the conduct of the directors against the statutory criteria for unfitness and considered the appropriate period of disqualification. The court applied the established framework of three brackets: 2-5 years for relatively minor misconduct; 6-10 years for serious cases not meriting the top bracket; and 11-15 years for the most serious cases. The judgment provides modern guidance on the assessment of unfitness and the calibration of disqualification periods.

legal_principle: The rule in Foss v. Harbottle provides that: (1) the proper plaintiff in an action for a wrong done to a company is the company itself (the proper plaintiff rule); and (2) the court will not interfere with a matter that can be ratified by a simple majority of shareholders (the majority rule). Exceptions exist for ultra vires acts, fraud on the minority, and infringement of personal rights.
key_quote: If a thing is done irregularly which the majority of the company are entitled to do regularly, the court will not interfere.
full_summary: Two shareholders of the Victoria Park Company brought an action alleging that the directors had misapplied the company's property. Wigram V-C held that the proper plaintiff in an action in respect of a wrong done to the company is the company itself, not individual shareholders. This established the proper plaintiff rule. Additionally, where the alleged wrong is an irregularity that could be ratified by a simple majority of shareholders, the court will not interfere at the instance of a minority shareholder. The rule has well-established exceptions: (1) ultra vires or illegal acts; (2) acts requiring a special majority; (3) fraud on the minority where the wrongdoers control the company; and (4) infringement of personal rights. The rule remains the starting point for all company litigation and is fundamental to FE-1 company law.

legal_principle: Irish Supreme Court authority on the rule in Foss v. Harbottle and the derivative action. A minority shareholder may bring a derivative action on behalf of the company where the wrongdoers are in control of the company and the wrong amounts to a fraud on the minority.
key_quote: A minority shareholder may bring a derivative action where the alleged wrongdoers are in control of the company and the acts complained of amount to a fraud on the minority.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the circumstances in which a minority shareholder may bring proceedings on behalf of the company (a derivative action) as an exception to the rule in Foss v. Harbottle. The court examined the fraud on the minority exception, which permits a minority shareholder to sue where the wrongdoers are in control of the company and the acts complained of are of a fraudulent character or constitute an abuse of the majority's power. The Supreme Court confirmed the applicability of the Foss v. Harbottle exceptions in Irish law and set out the requirements for bringing a derivative action. This is the leading Irish authority on derivative actions.

case_name: Prudential Assurance Co Ltd v. Newman Industries Ltd (No 2)
legal_principle: The fraud on the minority exception to the rule in Foss v. Harbottle requires the plaintiff to establish a prima facie case that: (1) the wrongdoers are in control of the company; and (2) the acts complained of constitute a fraud on the minority. The derivative action is subject to strict procedural requirements.
key_quote: A minority shareholder who brings a derivative action must establish as a preliminary matter that the case falls within the proper boundaries of the exception to the rule in Foss v. Harbottle.
full_summary: Prudential, a minority shareholder, brought proceedings alleging that the directors had caused the company to overpay for assets in a transaction that benefited the directors personally. The Court of Appeal examined the procedural requirements for derivative actions, holding that the minority shareholder must establish a prima facie case that the action falls within one of the exceptions to the rule in Foss v. Harbottle. The court held that to invoke the fraud on the minority exception, the plaintiff must show that the wrongdoers are in control of the company and that the acts complained of constitute a fraud (not limited to common law fraud but including any abuse of the majority's power). The case established important procedural requirements for derivative actions.

case_name: Scottish Co-operative Wholesale Society v. Meyer
legal_principle: The House of Lords upheld a finding of oppression where the majority shareholder deliberately ran down the company's business to force the minority to sell at a reduced price. The court ordered the majority to purchase the minority's shares at a fair value.
key_quote: The majority shareholders conducted the company's affairs in a manner oppressive to the minority by deliberately running down the company's business.
full_summary: Scottish Co-operative held the majority of shares in a textile company. When government policy changed, making the subsidiary less useful to the parent, Scottish Co-operative diverted business away from the subsidiary and transferred its customers to the parent, deliberately running down the subsidiary's business. The minority shareholders petitioned for relief from oppression. The House of Lords upheld the finding of oppression, holding that the deliberate conduct of the company's affairs in a manner calculated to destroy the value of the minority's shares constituted oppression. The court ordered the majority to purchase the minority's shares at a fair value that reflected the company's value before the oppressive conduct began.

legal_principle: Irish authority on oppression of minority shareholders. The court considered the scope of the oppression remedy under the Companies Act and the factors relevant to determining whether the affairs of the company have been conducted in a manner oppressive to a member.
key_quote: Oppression involves a visible departure from the standards of fair dealing and a violation of the conditions of fair play on which every shareholder is entitled to rely.
full_summary: A minority shareholder petitioned for relief on the grounds that the company's affairs were being conducted in a manner oppressive to him. The High Court examined the scope of the oppression remedy (now s.212 of the Companies Act 2014) and the meaning of oppression in the context of Irish company law. The court considered whether the conduct complained of amounted to a visible departure from the standards of fair dealing and a violation of the conditions of fair play on which every shareholder is entitled to rely. The judgment set out the factors relevant to the assessment of oppression and the remedies available, including the power to order the purchase of shares.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the oppression remedy. The exclusion of a director/shareholder from the management of the company may constitute oppression where the shareholder had a legitimate expectation of participation in management.
key_quote: The exclusion of a shareholder from the management of a company may constitute oppression where that shareholder had a legitimate expectation of involvement in the company's affairs.
full_summary: The petitioner was a director and shareholder who had been excluded from the management of the company. The High Court examined whether this exclusion constituted oppression within the meaning of the Companies Act. The court held that in a quasi-partnership company where the shareholder had a legitimate expectation of participation in management, exclusion from management may amount to oppression. The judgment applied the concept of legitimate expectations to the oppression remedy and is an important Irish authority on the rights of shareholders in quasi-partnership companies.

case_name: Re Irish Visiting Motorists' Bureau Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the oppression remedy. The diversion of business from the company to another entity controlled by the majority shareholder may constitute oppression of the minority.
key_quote: The diversion of the company's business to a rival entity controlled by the majority constitutes a clear case of oppression.
full_summary: The minority shareholder petitioned for relief on the grounds that the majority shareholder had diverted business from the company to another entity under the majority's control. The High Court held that the systematic diversion of the company's business opportunities to a rival entity controlled by the majority shareholder constituted oppression of the minority within the meaning of the Companies Act. The court considered the duty of the majority to act fairly towards the minority and the principle that the company's business should be conducted for the benefit of all shareholders. The case is an important Irish authority on the diversion of business as a form of oppression.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the oppression remedy in the context of a quasi-partnership company. Where the company is in substance a partnership and one partner is excluded, this may constitute oppression.
key_quote: In a quasi-partnership company, the exclusion of one partner from the affairs of the company may constitute oppression.
full_summary: The petitioner was a founding member of a company that operated a restaurant. The company had the characteristics of a quasi-partnership. The petitioner was excluded from the management and affairs of the company. The High Court held that where a company has the characteristics of a quasi-partnership (personal relationship of mutual trust and confidence, agreement that all members will participate in management, restriction on share transfer), the exclusion of a member from management may constitute oppression. The court ordered appropriate relief to vindicate the petitioner's rights. This is a leading Irish case on quasi-partnership companies and the oppression remedy.

legal_principle: Irish authority on oppression. The court may grant wide-ranging remedies under the oppression provisions, including ordering the purchase of shares, regulating the company's future conduct, or appointing a receiver.
key_quote: The court has a wide discretion as to the remedy to be granted in oppression proceedings.
full_summary: The minority shareholder petitioned for relief under the oppression provisions. The High Court examined the range of remedies available under the statutory oppression remedy (now s.212 of the Companies Act 2014) and the court's broad discretion in fashioning appropriate relief. The judgment addressed the circumstances in which the court may order the purchase of shares at a fair value (with or without a minority discount), regulate the future conduct of the company's affairs, or grant other appropriate relief. The case illustrates the flexibility of the oppression remedy in Irish law.

legal_principle: The oppression remedy protects shareholders from conduct that departs from the standards of fair dealing. The test is objective: would a reasonable bystander regard the conduct as unfairly prejudicial to the petitioner's interests?
key_quote: The test for oppression is whether the conduct of the affairs of the company is such that a reasonable bystander would regard it as unfairly prejudicial.
full_summary: The minority shareholder brought an oppression petition under the Companies Act. The High Court considered the test for oppression in Irish company law, holding that the question is whether the conduct of the company's affairs departs from the standards of fair dealing and is such that a reasonable bystander would regard it as unfairly prejudicial to the petitioner's interests as a member. The judgment examined the objective nature of the test and the factors relevant to the assessment of oppressive conduct. The case is cited as an Irish authority on the meaning of oppression and the standard against which the majority's conduct is measured.

case_name: Re Sam Weller and Sons Ltd
legal_principle: The persistent failure to pay dividends despite the company having substantial reserves may constitute unfairly prejudicial conduct, particularly where the majority directors are extracting value through their remuneration packages.
key_quote: The persistent failure to pay dividends where the company has ample resources may amount to unfairly prejudicial conduct.
full_summary: A minority shareholder petitioned for relief on the basis that the company had never paid a dividend despite having substantial reserves and distributable profits. The directors, who were the majority shareholders, were extracting value from the company through directors' remuneration. The court held that the persistent failure to pay dividends in these circumstances could constitute unfairly prejudicial conduct. The minority shareholder was effectively excluded from any benefit from the company's profits while the majority extracted value through their employment. The case illustrates the principle that oppression or unfair prejudice may arise from non-payment of dividends where the majority benefits through other means.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the oppression remedy. The court considered the circumstances in which the conduct of the company's affairs crosses the threshold from poor management to oppression.
key_quote: There is a distinction between mismanagement and oppression; not every instance of poor management amounts to oppression.
full_summary: The minority shareholder petitioned for relief under the oppression provisions. The High Court examined the distinction between mere mismanagement (which does not constitute oppression) and conduct that crosses the threshold into oppression. The court held that while poor business decisions may not amount to oppression, a pattern of conduct that demonstrates a disregard for the interests of the minority and a departure from the standards of fair dealing may cross the line. The judgment illustrates the threshold that must be met before the court will intervene under the oppression remedy.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the oppression remedy in the context of family companies. The exclusion of a family member from the management of a family company may constitute oppression where there was an understanding of participation.
key_quote: In a family company, exclusion from management contrary to an understanding of participation may constitute oppression.
full_summary: The case involved a family company where a member of the family had been excluded from management. The High Court examined whether the exclusion constituted oppression in the context of a family company where there was an understanding that family members would participate in management. The court applied the quasi-partnership principles and the concept of legitimate expectations, holding that in a company where the shareholders had an understanding of mutual participation in management, exclusion of a member may constitute oppression. The case illustrates the application of oppression principles to family-owned companies.

legal_principle: For conduct to constitute unfair prejudice, it must be both unfair and prejudicial. The court must identify the prejudice suffered by the petitioner and determine whether the conduct causing the prejudice was unfair.
key_quote: The conduct must be both unfair and prejudicial; neither element alone is sufficient.
full_summary: The minority shareholder petitioned for relief on the grounds that the company's affairs had been conducted in a manner unfairly prejudicial to his interests. Warner J held that the petitioner must establish both unfairness and prejudice. The court examined the meaning of each element: prejudice relates to actual damage to the petitioner's interests as a member, while unfairness requires a departure from the standards of commercial fairness applicable between the parties. The judgment provided guidance on the assessment of unfairly prejudicial conduct and the relationship between unfairness and prejudice.

legal_principle: The unfair prejudice remedy encompasses conduct that is commercially unfair. In assessing unfair prejudice, the court considers the legitimate expectations of the parties arising from their association, including informal understandings.
key_quote: Conduct that is commercially unfair, even if lawful, may amount to unfairly prejudicial conduct.
full_summary: The minority shareholder petitioned on the grounds that the majority had conducted the company's affairs in a manner that was unfairly prejudicial. The court held that the unfair prejudice remedy is broader than the oppression remedy and encompasses conduct that is commercially unfair even if technically lawful. The court considered the legitimate expectations of the parties arising from their association in the company, including informal understandings and arrangements that went beyond the strict terms of the articles. The judgment is important for its analysis of the role of legitimate expectations in unfair prejudice proceedings and the breadth of the remedy.

legal_principle: The case established the characteristics of a floating charge: (1) it is a charge on a class of assets present and future; (2) the class is one which in the ordinary course of business changes from time to time; and (3) the company may deal with the assets in the ordinary course of business until crystallisation.
key_quote: A floating charge is ambulatory and shifting in its nature, hovering over and so to speak floating with the property which it is intended to affect until some event occurs which causes it to settle and fasten on the subject of the charge.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the nature and characteristics of a floating charge. Romer LJ set out the three characteristics that distinguish a floating charge from a fixed charge: (1) it is a charge on a class of assets of the company, present and future; (2) that class of assets is one which, in the ordinary course of the company's business, would change from time to time; and (3) it is contemplated that, until some future step is taken by or on behalf of the chargee, the company may carry on its business in the ordinary way and deal with the charged assets. These three characteristics remain the definitive test for identifying a floating charge and are central to FE-1 company law.

legal_principle: A fixed charge attaches to specific, ascertained property and the chargor cannot deal with the property without the chargee's consent. A floating charge does not attach to specific property until crystallisation and allows the chargor to deal with the charged assets.
key_quote: A specific charge is one that without more fastens on ascertained and definite property; a floating charge is ambulatory and hovers over the property until crystallisation.
full_summary: The House of Lords distinguished between fixed and floating charges. Lord Macnaghten held that a specific (fixed) charge is one that attaches to ascertained and definite property without more, preventing the chargor from dealing with the property without the chargee's consent. A floating charge, by contrast, is ambulatory and shifting, hovering over the company's property until an event causes it to crystallise and attach to specific assets. The distinction is fundamental because fixed charges have priority over floating charges and floating charges are subordinated to preferential creditors in a winding up. The case is the leading House of Lords authority on the distinction between fixed and floating charges.

case_name: Siebe Gorman and Co Ltd v. Barclays Bank Ltd
legal_principle: A charge over book debts may be a fixed charge if the chargor is required to pay the proceeds into a designated bank account and is restricted from dealing with those proceeds without the chargee's consent.
key_quote: The essential characteristic of a fixed charge is that the chargor has no freedom to deal with the asset subject to the charge without the chargee's consent.
full_summary: The question was whether a charge over book debts was a fixed or floating charge. Slade J held that the charge was a fixed charge because the debenture required the company to pay the proceeds of the book debts into a designated bank account with the chargee bank and restricted the company from dealing with those proceeds without the bank's consent. The court held that the critical test was whether the chargor retained freedom to deal with the charged assets: if the chargor was restricted from dealing with the assets, the charge was fixed; if the chargor could deal with the assets in the ordinary course of business, the charge was floating. This decision was subsequently doubted by the House of Lords in Spectrum Plus.

case_name: National Westminster Bank plc v. Spectrum Plus Ltd
legal_principle: The House of Lords held that a charge over book debts cannot be a fixed charge unless the chargor is actually restricted from dealing with the proceeds. The label attached by the parties to the charge is not determinative; the court must examine the substance of the arrangement.
key_quote: The essential question is whether the chargor has the ability to control the use of the charged assets. The label given to the charge by the parties is not conclusive.
full_summary: The House of Lords considered whether a charge over book debts was a fixed or floating charge. The debenture purported to create a fixed charge over the company's book debts and required proceeds to be paid into a designated account, but in practice the company was free to draw on the account without restriction. The House of Lords (overruling Siebe Gorman) held that the charge was a floating charge because the company retained the ability to deal with the proceeds of the book debts without the bank's consent. The label attached to the charge by the parties was not determinative; the court must look at the substance of the arrangement and in particular whether the chargor retained control over the charged assets. This is the definitive authority on the classification of charges over book debts.

legal_principle: Leading Irish authority on the distinction between fixed and floating charges. The Supreme Court held that a charge over book debts was a fixed charge where the company was required to pay proceeds into a blocked account from which withdrawals could only be made with the chargee's consent.
key_quote: Where the chargor has no freedom to deal with the proceeds of book debts without the chargee's consent, the charge is a fixed charge.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered whether a charge created by a debenture over the book debts of Keenan Brothers was a fixed or floating charge. The debenture required the company to pay the proceeds of book debts into a designated account with the chargee bank, and the company could only withdraw funds from that account with the bank's written consent. The Supreme Court held that the charge was a fixed charge because the company had no freedom to deal with the proceeds of the book debts without the bank's consent. The decisive factor was the actual restriction on the company's ability to deal with the charged assets. This remains the leading Irish authority on fixed charges over book debts, though its compatibility with the later Spectrum Plus decision has been debated.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the registration of charges. Failure to register a charge within the statutory time limit renders the charge void against the liquidator and creditors. The court has a discretion to extend the time for registration.
key_quote: A charge that is not registered within the prescribed period is void against the liquidator and creditors of the company.
full_summary: The case concerned the consequences of failure to register a charge within the time prescribed by the Companies Act. The High Court held that failure to register renders the charge void against the liquidator and any creditor of the company. The court examined the statutory framework for the registration of charges and the circumstances in which the court may exercise its discretion to extend the time for registration. The judgment addressed the policy rationale for the registration requirement (protecting creditors by ensuring transparency about the company's security arrangements) and the factors relevant to the exercise of the court's discretion to extend time.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court addressed the priority of charges and the treatment of floating charges in a winding up. Floating charges rank behind fixed charges and preferential creditors. The case clarified the distinction between fixed and floating charges in Irish law.
key_quote: In a winding up, a floating charge ranks behind fixed charges and preferential creditors.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the priority of charges in the winding up of Holidair Ltd. The court examined the distinction between fixed and floating charges and the priority rules applicable in a winding up. The Supreme Court confirmed that floating charges rank behind fixed charges and preferential creditors in the distribution of the company's assets. The judgment addressed the characteristics of each type of charge, the circumstances in which a floating charge crystallises, and the effect of crystallisation on priority. The decision is a leading Irish authority on the priority of charges and the treatment of floating charges in insolvency.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court addressed the status of Revenue as a preferential creditor in a winding up and the interaction between Revenue claims and floating charges. The case clarified the ranking of preferential debts in Irish insolvency law.
key_quote: Revenue debts retain their preferential status in a winding up and rank ahead of the claims of floating charge holders.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the status of Revenue claims in the winding up of JD Brian Ltd (also known as the Belgard Motors case). The court examined the ranking of preferential debts, including Revenue claims, in the distribution of assets realised under a floating charge. The Supreme Court confirmed that Revenue debts retain their preferential status and rank ahead of the claims of floating charge holders. The judgment clarified the interaction between the statutory preferential creditor regime and the rights of floating charge holders, and addressed the practical consequences for secured creditors in Irish insolvencies.

case_name: Smith v. Bridgend County Borough Council
legal_principle: The crystallisation of a floating charge converts it into a fixed charge over the assets within the scope of the charge at the date of crystallisation. After crystallisation, the company can no longer deal with the charged assets in the ordinary course of business.
key_quote: Upon crystallisation, a floating charge ceases to float and attaches to the assets then comprised in the charge.
full_summary: The House of Lords considered the effect of the crystallisation of a floating charge on the company's ability to deal with the charged assets. The court held that crystallisation converts a floating charge into a fixed charge over the assets within the scope of the charge as at the date of crystallisation. After crystallisation, the company is no longer free to deal with the charged assets in the ordinary course of business. The judgment addressed the events that trigger crystallisation (including appointment of a receiver, commencement of winding up, and automatic crystallisation clauses) and the practical consequences of crystallisation for both the company and third parties.

legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on the registration and enforcement of charges under the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the requirements for valid creation and registration of charges.
key_quote: A charge must be properly created and registered to be enforceable against the company's creditors and liquidator.
full_summary: The High Court addressed questions concerning the creation and registration of charges under Part 7 of the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the requirements for the valid creation of a charge, the formalities required for registration, and the consequences of failure to register. The judgment provided modern guidance on the application of the charging provisions of the 2014 Act and their interaction with the common law principles governing fixed and floating charges. The case is a contemporary Irish authority on the charging regime under the Companies Act 2014.

legal_principle: Where a director enters into a substantial property transaction with the company without the required shareholder approval, the transaction is voidable and the director must account to the company for any gain and indemnify the company for any loss.
key_quote: A substantial property transaction that does not receive the required shareholder approval is voidable at the instance of the company.
full_summary: The case concerned a substantial property transaction between a director and the company that had not received the required shareholder approval under what is now s.238 of the UK Companies Act 2006 (equivalent to s.238 of the Irish Companies Act 2014). The Court of Appeal held that such a transaction is voidable at the instance of the company. The court addressed the remedies available where a substantial property transaction is entered into without approval, including the right to have the transaction set aside and the right to require the director to account for any gain and indemnify the company for any loss. The judgment clarified the consequences of non-compliance with the substantial property transaction requirements.

case_name: Joint Receivers and Managers of Niltan Carson Ltd v. Hawthorne
legal_principle: The powers of a receiver appointed under a debenture are determined by the terms of the debenture and the applicable statute. A receiver acts primarily in the interest of the debenture holder, not the company.
key_quote: A receiver appointed under a debenture acts primarily in the interests of the debenture holder who appointed him.
full_summary: The case addressed the powers and duties of receivers appointed under a debenture. The court examined the scope of the receiver's authority as defined by the terms of the debenture and the applicable statutory provisions. The judgment confirmed that a receiver appointed under a debenture acts primarily in the interest of the debenture holder (the secured creditor) and not in the interest of the company or its unsecured creditors. However, the receiver owes certain duties to the company, including a duty to exercise reasonable care in the disposal of the company's assets. The case is an authority on the position and duties of receivers in company law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the relationship between directors and receivers. Where a receiver is appointed, the directors' powers of management are suspended in respect of the assets in receivership, but the directors remain in office.
key_quote: The appointment of a receiver suspends the directors' powers of management over the assets subject to the receivership.
full_summary: The case addressed the relationship between the directors of a company and a receiver appointed over the company's assets. The High Court examined the effect of a receiver's appointment on the powers and duties of the directors. The court held that while the appointment of a receiver suspends the directors' powers of management in respect of the assets in receivership, the directors remain in office and retain their powers in relation to matters not affected by the receivership. The judgment is an Irish authority on the interaction between the receivership and the directors' powers.

case_name: Ruby Property Co v. Kilty and Superquinn
legal_principle: Irish authority on the appointment and powers of receivers. The court examined the circumstances in which a receiver may be appointed and the duties owed by a receiver in the exercise of the power of sale.
key_quote: A receiver exercising the power of sale owes a duty to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable.
full_summary: The case concerned the appointment and conduct of a receiver over property. The High Court examined the circumstances in which a receiver may be appointed under a debenture and the duties owed by the receiver, particularly in relation to the exercise of the power of sale. The court held that a receiver exercising the power of sale owes a duty to the company (and to subsequent encumbrancers and the company's creditors) to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable. The judgment addressed the standard of care applicable to receivers in the disposition of the company's assets.

case_name: AIB Bank plc v. Ardmore Studios International Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the appointment of receivers by banks under debentures. The court examined the circumstances justifying the appointment of a receiver and the bank's entitlement to enforce its security.
key_quote: A bank is entitled to appoint a receiver under its debenture where the conditions for appointment specified in the debenture have been met.
full_summary: AIB Bank sought to appoint a receiver over the assets of Ardmore Studios under the terms of a debenture. The High Court examined the circumstances justifying the appointment, including the occurrence of events of default specified in the debenture. The court held that where the conditions for appointment specified in the debenture have been met, the bank is entitled to appoint a receiver to enforce its security. The judgment addressed the contractual basis for the appointment of receivers and the relationship between the bank, the receiver, and the company.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court established the principles governing fraudulent preference in Irish company law. A disposition of company property made with the dominant intention of giving a preference to one creditor over others is a fraudulent preference and may be set aside by the liquidator.
key_quote: The dominant intention test requires the court to examine whether the primary purpose of the payment was to prefer one creditor over others.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the law on fraudulent preference in the context of a company in liquidation. The liquidator sought to set aside payments made by the company to certain creditors in the period before liquidation, arguing these were fraudulent preferences. The Supreme Court applied the dominant intention test, holding that a disposition is a fraudulent preference if the dominant intention was to give a preference to one creditor over others. The test is subjective: the court must examine the company's intention at the time of the payment. The judgment established the framework for analysing fraudulent preference claims in Irish company law and is the leading authority on this topic.

legal_principle: Directors who make distributions to shareholders out of capital rather than out of profits are in breach of their duty to maintain the company's capital. The directors may be personally liable to repay the amounts unlawfully distributed.
key_quote: Directors who pay dividends out of capital when they know or ought to know there are no profits available for distribution are liable to make good the amount improperly paid.
full_summary: Directors of the Exchange Banking Company paid dividends to shareholders when the company had no distributable profits, effectively making distributions out of capital. The Court of Appeal held that the directors were personally liable to make good the amounts improperly distributed. Directors who pay dividends when they know or ought to know that there are no profits available for distribution are in breach of their duty to maintain the company's capital. The case is a foundational authority on the capital maintenance doctrine and the prohibition on distributions out of capital, principles that are now codified in the Companies Act 2014.

case_name: Re Verit Hotel and Leisure (Ireland) Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties of liquidators and the process of winding up. The court examined the powers and responsibilities of the liquidator in managing the orderly realisation and distribution of the company's assets.
key_quote: The liquidator has a duty to realise the company's assets and distribute them in accordance with the statutory order of priority.
full_summary: The case addressed issues arising in the liquidation of a hotel company. The High Court examined the powers and duties of the liquidator in the winding up process, including the duty to realise the company's assets at the best price obtainable, to investigate the conduct of the directors, and to distribute the proceeds in accordance with the statutory order of priority. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of the winding up process and the court's supervisory role over the liquidator.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the grounds for compulsory winding up. The court may order the winding up of a company on the just and equitable ground where there has been a breakdown of trust and confidence among the members.
key_quote: The court may wind up a company on the just and equitable ground where the substratum of the company has gone or where there has been a complete breakdown of trust and confidence.
full_summary: The petition sought the compulsory winding up of the company on the just and equitable ground. The High Court examined the circumstances in which the court will exercise its jurisdiction to wind up a company on the just and equitable ground under what is now s.569(1)(f) of the Companies Act 2014. The court held that the just and equitable ground may be invoked where there has been a complete breakdown of trust and confidence among the members (particularly in a quasi-partnership company), where the substratum of the company has gone, or where the company's affairs have been conducted in a manner that justifies the court's intervention. The case illustrates the application of the just and equitable winding up jurisdiction.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the grounds for winding up on the inability to pay debts. A company may be wound up where it is unable to pay its debts as they fall due (the cash-flow test) or where its liabilities exceed its assets (the balance-sheet test).
key_quote: A company is deemed unable to pay its debts where it fails to satisfy a statutory demand or where it is proved to the satisfaction of the court that the company is unable to pay its debts.
full_summary: The petition sought the compulsory winding up of the company on the grounds of inability to pay debts. The High Court examined the statutory grounds for winding up under what is now s.569(1)(d) and s.570 of the Companies Act 2014. The court addressed the two tests for inability to pay debts: the cash-flow test (whether the company is unable to pay its debts as they fall due) and the balance-sheet test (whether the value of the company's assets is less than the amount of its liabilities, taking into account contingent and prospective liabilities). The judgment is an Irish authority on the statutory winding up grounds.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties and liabilities of directors in the context of reckless trading. Directors who allow the company to continue trading when they knew or ought to have known that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvency may be personally liable.
key_quote: A director who allows the company to continue trading with no reasonable prospect of meeting its debts may be liable for reckless trading.
full_summary: The liquidator brought proceedings against the directors for reckless trading under what is now s.610 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court examined the elements of reckless trading, including whether the directors were knowingly a party to the carrying on of the company's business in a reckless manner, and whether they ought to have known that their actions or inactions would cause loss to creditors. The court considered the standard of conduct expected of directors and the circumstances in which personal liability for the company's debts may be imposed. The judgment is an Irish authority on the reckless trading provisions.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the conduct of liquidations. The court addressed the liquidator's obligation to investigate the conduct of directors and to pursue claims on behalf of the company's creditors where appropriate.
key_quote: The liquidator has an obligation to investigate the conduct of the company's directors and to pursue claims where it is in the interest of the creditors to do so.
full_summary: The case arose in the context of the liquidation of a motor dealership. The High Court examined the liquidator's duties in the conduct of the winding up, including the obligation to investigate the conduct of the directors, to report any misconduct to the ODCE, and to pursue claims on behalf of the company's creditors. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of liquidation proceedings and the interaction between the liquidator's investigatory and recovery functions.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the context of winding up. The liquidator's obligation to apply for restriction ensures that directors of insolvent companies are assessed for their fitness to continue as directors.
key_quote: The liquidator is obliged to bring restriction applications to ensure that the court can assess the fitness of directors of insolvent companies.
full_summary: The case addressed the liquidator's statutory obligation to apply for the restriction of directors of an insolvent company. The High Court examined the mandatory nature of the restriction application and the factors the court considers in deciding whether directors have met the honest and responsible threshold. The judgment reinforced the principle that restriction is a protective measure designed to ensure that only fit and responsible persons participate in the management of companies.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the process of voluntary winding up. The court examined the distinction between members' voluntary winding up and creditors' voluntary winding up and the procedures applicable to each.
key_quote: The distinction between a members' and creditors' voluntary winding up depends on the solvency of the company at the time of the winding up resolution.
full_summary: The case addressed issues arising in the voluntary winding up of Connemara Mining Co. The High Court examined the statutory framework for voluntary winding up under the Companies Act, including the distinction between members' voluntary winding up (where the directors can make a declaration of solvency) and creditors' voluntary winding up (where no such declaration can be made). The judgment addressed the procedures applicable to each type of voluntary winding up and the consequences of the directors' declaration of solvency proving to be incorrect.

legal_principle: Leading Irish authority on examinership. The court examined the criteria for the appointment of an examiner, including the requirement that the company must have a reasonable prospect of survival as a going concern.
key_quote: The court will appoint an examiner only where it is satisfied that there is a reasonable prospect of the survival of the company as a going concern.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the appointment of an examiner to Bula Ltd under the Companies (Amendment) Act 1990 (now Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014). The court examined the criteria for the appointment of an examiner, particularly the requirement that the company must have a reasonable prospect of survival as a going concern. The Supreme Court held that the court must be satisfied on the evidence that there is a reasonable prospect that the company and all or part of its undertaking can survive as a going concern. The judgment is the foundational Irish authority on examinership and the survival test.

legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on the criteria for the appointment of an examiner. The court must consider whether there is a reasonable prospect of survival and whether the appointment would be likely to facilitate such survival.
key_quote: The appointment of an examiner must be likely to facilitate the survival of the company as a going concern.
full_summary: The High Court considered an application for the appointment of an examiner to Burren Springs Ltd. The court examined the statutory criteria for examinership under the Companies Act, including whether the company had a reasonable prospect of survival as a going concern and whether the appointment of an examiner would be likely to facilitate such survival. The judgment addressed the evidence required to satisfy the survival test, including the preparation of a report by an independent accountant. The case provides modern guidance on the practical requirements for an examinership petition.

case_name: In re North Brazilian Sugar Factories
legal_principle: A liquidator has the power to disclaim onerous property, including unprofitable contracts and property that is unsaleable or gives rise to liabilities. Disclaimer releases the company from liability but does not affect the rights of third parties who have claims against the company.
key_quote: The liquidator may disclaim onerous property where it would be to the benefit of the company's creditors as a whole.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal considered the liquidator's power to disclaim onerous property in a winding up. The case established the principles governing disclaimer, including the types of property that may be disclaimed (unprofitable contracts, unsaleable property, property giving rise to liabilities) and the consequences of disclaimer for the company and third parties. The court held that disclaimer releases the company from liability under the disclaimed contract or in respect of the disclaimed property but does not affect the rights of third parties to prove in the winding up for any loss suffered as a result of the disclaimer.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the application of examinership provisions under the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the threshold requirements and the protection of the examination period.
key_quote: During the examination period, the company is protected from enforcement of claims against it.
full_summary: The High Court considered an application for the appointment of an examiner under Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the statutory requirements for examinership, including the survival test and the requirement for an independent expert's report. The judgment addressed the effect of the appointment on the company's creditors and the protection afforded to the company during the examination period. The case provides guidance on the application of the examinership provisions under the 2014 Act.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the powers and duties of liquidators. The court examined the liquidator's duty to investigate and report on the conduct of the directors and the duty to apply for restriction.
key_quote: The liquidator must investigate the conduct of the directors and bring applications as required by statute.
full_summary: The case addressed the duties of the liquidator in the context of a compulsory winding up. The High Court examined the liquidator's statutory obligations, including the duty to take into custody and control all the company's property, to investigate the affairs of the company, to report on the conduct of the directors to the ODCE, and to bring applications for the restriction or disqualification of directors. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of the liquidator's role and the court's supervisory jurisdiction over liquidation proceedings.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the doctrine of fraudulent trading under company law. A person who is knowingly a party to the carrying on of the company's business with intent to defraud creditors may be held personally liable for the company's debts.
key_quote: Fraudulent trading requires proof of an intention to defraud creditors, which is a higher threshold than mere recklessness.
full_summary: The liquidator brought proceedings against the respondent for fraudulent trading under what is now s.722 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court examined the elements of fraudulent trading, including the requirement to prove that the respondent was knowingly a party to the carrying on of the company's business with intent to defraud creditors. The court distinguished between fraudulent trading (which requires proof of intent to defraud, a higher threshold) and reckless trading (which requires proof that the person ought to have known that their actions would cause loss). The judgment clarified the distinction between these two forms of personal liability.

case_name: Ryanair Ltd v. Aer Lingus Group plc
legal_principle: Irish authority on the proper purpose doctrine in the context of share allotments and takeover defences. Directors may not use their powers to allot shares for the primary purpose of defeating a takeover bid or diluting an existing shareholder's stake.
key_quote: The power to allot shares must be exercised for a proper purpose and not to dilute the voting power of an existing shareholder or to defeat a takeover.
full_summary: Ryanair challenged actions by Aer Lingus in the context of a takeover bid. The High Court examined the proper purpose doctrine as applied to share allotments and other corporate actions taken in the context of a contested takeover. The court applied the principles from Howard Smith v. Ampol, holding that directors must exercise their powers for the purposes for which they were conferred and not for the collateral purpose of entrenching themselves or defeating a legitimate takeover bid. The judgment is an important Irish authority on directors' duties in the takeover context.

case_name: Breckland Group Holdings Ltd v. London and Suffolk Properties Ltd
legal_principle: The general meeting cannot interfere with the exercise of powers that are vested in the directors by the articles of association. The division of powers between the board and the general meeting is determined by the articles.
key_quote: Where the articles vest the power of management in the directors, the general meeting cannot override the board's decision.
full_summary: The case concerned the division of powers between the board of directors and the general meeting. Harman J held that where the articles vest the power of management in the directors, the general meeting cannot override or interfere with the board's exercise of those powers. The shareholders' remedy, if dissatisfied with the directors' management, is to remove the directors by ordinary resolution, not to bypass the board and purport to exercise management powers themselves. The case is a leading authority on the separation of powers between the board and the general meeting.

case_name: Re Burke Clancy and Co Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the circumstances justifying the appointment of a provisional liquidator. The court may appoint a provisional liquidator where there is evidence of dissipation or jeopardy to the company's assets pending the hearing of the winding up petition.
key_quote: A provisional liquidator may be appointed where there is evidence of risk to the company's assets pending the hearing of the winding up petition.
full_summary: The petitioner sought the appointment of a provisional liquidator pending the hearing of a winding up petition. The High Court examined the circumstances justifying the appointment of a provisional liquidator, including evidence of actual or threatened dissipation of the company's assets, risk of destruction of records, or other circumstances demonstrating a need for the immediate protection of the company's property. The judgment set out the threshold that must be met and the factors the court considers in deciding whether to appoint a provisional liquidator.

case_name: Woldview Ltd v. Petroclectic Resources plc
legal_principle: Irish authority on minority shareholder rights and the obligation of the board to act in the interests of the company as a whole. The court examined the limits of the majority's power to override minority interests.
key_quote: The board must act in the interests of the company as a whole, which includes having regard to the interests of minority shareholders.
full_summary: The minority shareholder challenged certain corporate actions taken by the board, alleging that they were not in the interests of the company as a whole and were prejudicial to the minority. The High Court examined the duties of the board to act in the interests of the company as a whole and the extent to which minority shareholders are entitled to protection from unfair conduct. The judgment applied the principles of the oppression remedy and considered the relationship between the board's duty to the company and its obligations towards minority shareholders.

case_name: Media Communication Consultants Ltd v. Baxter
legal_principle: Irish authority on directors' duties in the context of corporate transactions. The court examined the duty of directors to act in good faith and for a proper purpose when entering into transactions on behalf of the company.
key_quote: Directors must ensure that corporate transactions are entered into in good faith and for the benefit of the company.
full_summary: The case concerned corporate transactions entered into by the directors. The High Court examined whether the directors had acted in good faith and for a proper purpose. The court applied the established principles of directors' fiduciary duties, including the duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company and the duty not to exercise powers for an improper purpose. The judgment is cited as an Irish authority on the application of fiduciary principles to corporate transactions.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the conduct of liquidation proceedings. The court examined the duties and powers of liquidators in pursuing misfeasance claims against directors.
key_quote: The liquidator has a duty to pursue claims against directors where the recovery would benefit the creditors.
full_summary: The liquidator brought misfeasance proceedings against the directors. The High Court examined the scope of misfeasance proceedings in the context of a winding up and the duties of the liquidator in pursuing such claims. The court considered the circumstances in which a director may be liable for misfeasance (breach of fiduciary duty, negligence, or other default in the course of managing the company) and the remedies available. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of misfeasance proceedings and the standard of proof required.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court examined whether directors of a small company had acted honestly and responsibly, considering the particular challenges facing small businesses.
key_quote: The standard of responsible conduct must be assessed in context, taking into account the size and resources of the company.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of the directors of a small design company. The High Court examined the directors' conduct against the honest and responsible standard, taking into account the particular challenges facing directors of small businesses, including limited resources, reliance on key customers, and the difficulties of maintaining compliance with all statutory obligations. The judgment illustrates the proportionate application of the restriction test to directors of small companies.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court examined the totality of the directors' conduct and assessed whether the directors had demonstrated responsible behaviour throughout the life of the company.
key_quote: The court assesses the directors' conduct over the entire life of the company, not merely in the period immediately before insolvency.
full_summary: The liquidator applied to restrict the directors. The High Court examined the directors' conduct throughout the company's life, not just in the period immediately before the onset of insolvency. The court considered compliance with statutory obligations, maintenance of books and records, co-operation with the liquidator, and the general standard of management. The judgment reinforces the principle that the assessment of responsible conduct is holistic and considers the director's behaviour throughout the company's existence.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties and liabilities of directors in the context of company failure. The court examined the circumstances in which directors may be personally liable for the debts of the company.
key_quote: Directors may be personally liable where they have acted recklessly or fraudulently in the conduct of the company's affairs.
full_summary: The liquidator brought proceedings against the directors seeking personal liability for the company's debts on the grounds of reckless or fraudulent trading. The High Court examined the statutory provisions on reckless and fraudulent trading and the circumstances in which personal liability may be imposed on directors. The court considered the directors' conduct in the period leading up to the company's insolvency and whether they had allowed the company to continue trading when they knew or ought to have known that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvent liquidation.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the winding up of companies and the assessment of directors' conduct. The case examined the obligation of the liquidator to investigate and report on the directors' management of the company.
key_quote: The liquidator must investigate the conduct of the directors and make appropriate applications to the court.
full_summary: The case arose in the winding up of a cinema group. The High Court examined the liquidator's duties in investigating the conduct of the directors and the circumstances in which restriction or disqualification applications should be brought. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of the liquidator's investigatory function and the factors relevant to the assessment of the directors' management of the company.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of shareholders' agreements and the relationship between shareholders' agreements and the articles of association.
key_quote: A shareholders' agreement operates as a contract between the parties and may supplement or qualify the provisions of the articles.
full_summary: The case concerned the enforcement of a shareholders' agreement and its interaction with the articles of association. The High Court examined the legal nature of shareholders' agreements, holding that they operate as ordinary contracts between the parties and may supplement or qualify the provisions of the articles. However, a shareholders' agreement cannot override mandatory provisions of the Companies Act. The judgment addressed the remedies available for breach of a shareholders' agreement and the circumstances in which specific performance may be ordered.

legal_principle: A director cannot purchase the company's property from the company where he is interested in the transaction, unless the company gives its informed consent. The transaction is voidable at the company's option.
key_quote: A person standing in a fiduciary position may not purchase from himself.
full_summary: Farrar was a mortgagee who sold the mortgaged property to a company of which he was a director. The Court of Appeal held that a director cannot purchase the company's property (or sell his own property to the company) without full disclosure and the company's informed consent. Where a director has a personal interest in a transaction with the company, the transaction is voidable at the instance of the company. The self-dealing rule prevents directors from being on both sides of a transaction unless the company has given its informed consent after full disclosure.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of bank security over company assets and the priority of claims in insolvency. The court addressed the rights of a secured creditor to enforce its security against the company.
key_quote: A secured creditor is entitled to enforce its security over the company's assets in priority to unsecured creditors.
full_summary: ACC Bank brought proceedings to enforce its security over the company's assets. The High Court examined the rights of a secured creditor to enforce its security, including the appointment of a receiver and the right to proceed against the charged assets. The court addressed the priority of the bank's secured claim over the claims of unsecured creditors and the statutory framework governing the enforcement of security in the context of company insolvency.

case_name: Roma Corporation Pty Ltd v. Proved Tin and General Investments Ltd
legal_principle: A company's agent must have actual or apparent authority to bind the company. The scope of an agent's authority depends on the terms of appointment and the nature of the agent's position.
key_quote: An agent's authority to bind the company is limited to the scope of his appointment and the customary authority of his position.
full_summary: The case addressed the scope of an agent's authority to bind a company. The Supreme Court of Victoria examined the principles of actual and apparent authority in the context of a mining company. The court held that the agent's authority to bind the company was limited to the scope of his appointment and the customary authority associated with his position. Transactions entered into by the agent outside the scope of his authority were not binding on the company unless ratified. The case illustrates the limits of corporate agency.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the legal effects of incorporation. Once incorporated, the company has a legal existence separate from its members and can sue and be sued in its own name.
key_quote: Incorporation creates a legal person distinct from its members.
full_summary: The High Court addressed the consequences of incorporation, confirming that once a company is incorporated it has a separate legal existence from its members. The company can own property, enter into contracts, sue and be sued in its own name. The case applied the fundamental Salomon principle in the Irish context and addressed the practical implications of separate legal personality for the conduct of litigation.

case_name: Donegal Investment Group plc v. Danbywiske
legal_principle: The Supreme Court addressed the scope of discovery in company law litigation and the rights of shareholders to obtain information about the company's affairs through litigation. The proportionality of discovery orders must be assessed.
key_quote: Discovery in company litigation must be proportionate to the issues in dispute and the legitimate interests of the parties.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered an appeal relating to discovery in the context of company law litigation. The court examined the scope of discovery available to shareholders and the principles governing the proportionality of discovery orders. The judgment addressed the balance between the shareholder's right to obtain relevant information about the company's affairs and the company's right to protect confidential business information. The Supreme Court held that discovery must be proportionate to the issues in dispute and relevant to the legal questions to be determined.

legal_principle: Irish authority on oppression proceedings in the context of family-owned companies. The court examined the application of the s.212 oppression remedy where family relationships have broken down.
key_quote: In a family-owned company, the breakdown of family relationships may give rise to conduct that constitutes oppression.
full_summary: The petitioner brought oppression proceedings under s.212 of the Companies Act 2014 in the context of a family-owned company where relationships had broken down. The High Court examined the application of the oppression remedy to family companies, considering the legitimate expectations arising from the family relationship and the effect of the breakdown on the management of the company. The judgment addressed the remedies available, including the order for the purchase of shares at a fair value.

case_name: Re Alvonway Investments Ltd; Fennell v. O'Donovan
legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on restriction proceedings under the Companies Act 2014. The court applied the honest and responsible test to the directors' conduct.
key_quote: The honest and responsible test must be applied to the totality of the directors' conduct over the life of the company.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court applied the honest and responsible test, examining the directors' compliance with statutory obligations, maintenance of proper books of account, tax compliance, and general management of the company. The judgment provides a modern application of the restriction provisions under the 2014 Act.

legal_principle: Irish authority on examinership. The court examined the criteria for the appointment of an examiner and the formulation of a scheme of arrangement that is fair to all classes of creditors.
key_quote: A scheme of arrangement proposed by the examiner must be fair and equitable to all classes of creditors.
full_summary: The High Court considered the examinership of Via Net Works (Ireland) Ltd. The court examined the requirements for a scheme of arrangement proposed by the examiner, including the requirement that the scheme be fair and equitable to all classes of creditors. The judgment addressed the process by which the examiner formulates proposals for a compromise or scheme of arrangement, the requirement for court approval, and the factors the court considers in deciding whether to confirm the scheme. The case is an important Irish authority on the practical operation of the examinership process.

case_name: Meridian Motors Ltd (In Liquidation) v. Companies Act 2014
legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court examined the application of s.819 of the Companies Act 2014 to directors of a motor dealership in liquidation.
key_quote: The court must assess whether the directors' conduct meets the statutory threshold of honesty and responsibility.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Meridian Motors, a motor dealership in liquidation. The High Court applied the restriction criteria under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014, examining the directors' management of the company, compliance with statutory obligations, and the circumstances leading to insolvency. The judgment provides a recent application of the restriction provisions.

case_name: Inch View Ltd (In Liquidation) v. Companies Act 2014
legal_principle: 2022 Irish authority on the restriction of directors under the Companies Act 2014. The court examined contemporary standards of responsible conduct expected of directors.
key_quote: Directors must meet the contemporary standards of responsible conduct as required by the Companies Act 2014.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Inch View Ltd. The High Court applied the restriction criteria under s.819, examining the directors' conduct against contemporary standards of responsible management. The judgment is a recent application of the restriction provisions and provides guidance on the current expectations of directors of companies in liquidation.

case_name: Eurosurgical Ltd (In Liquidation) v. Companies Act 2014
legal_principle: 2022 Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of a surgical supplies company.
key_quote: The assessment of responsible conduct takes into account the nature of the company's business and the regulatory environment in which it operates.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Eurosurgical Ltd. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of a company operating in the regulated healthcare sector. The court considered the particular obligations and standards applicable to directors of companies in regulated industries. The judgment provides recent guidance on the application of restriction provisions to companies in specialised sectors.

case_name: Re Red Herring Seafood Ltd; Kennedy v. Aziz and Baig
legal_principle: 2022 Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court examined the directors' conduct in relation to a seafood company, assessing compliance with employment law, tax obligations, and proper management.
key_quote: Directors must comply with all relevant statutory obligations, including employment law and tax requirements, to demonstrate responsible conduct.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Red Herring Seafood Ltd. The High Court examined the directors' conduct across multiple dimensions, including compliance with employment law obligations, tax compliance, maintenance of books and records, and the general management of the company. The judgment illustrates the breadth of factors the court considers in restriction proceedings and the importance of compliance across all regulatory areas.

case_name: Compagnie Financiere et Commerciale du Pacifique v. Peruvian Guano Co
legal_principle: The leading authority on the test for relevance in discovery applications. A document is discoverable if it is directly relevant to the issues in the action or if it may lead to a train of inquiry that may directly or indirectly assist the party's case.
key_quote: A document is discoverable if it may lead to a train of inquiry which may directly or indirectly assist the party.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal established the test for relevance in discovery applications. Brett LJ held that a document is discoverable if it is directly relevant to the matters in issue in the action, or if it may lead to a train of inquiry which may either directly or indirectly enable the party to advance his own case or damage the case of his adversary. This broad test for discovery has been widely applied in company law litigation, particularly in derivative actions, oppression proceedings, and directors' duties cases. The case remains the leading authority on the scope of discovery.

legal_principle: Irish Supreme Court authority on the enforcement of company law and the duties of promoters. Promoters owe fiduciary duties to the company being promoted.
key_quote: A promoter owes a fiduciary duty to the company he is engaged in promoting.
full_summary: The Supreme Court examined the duties of promoters in Irish company law. The court held that a person who acts as a promoter of a company owes fiduciary duties to the company, including a duty to make full disclosure of all material facts and a duty not to make a secret profit at the company's expense. The judgment addressed the circumstances in which a promoter may be held liable for breach of fiduciary duty and the remedies available to the company. The case is the leading Irish authority on the fiduciary duties of promoters.

case_name: Sheeran, Murphy and Mulcahy v. Fitzpatrick
legal_principle: 2021 Irish authority on oppression and minority shareholder disputes. The High Court considered the application of s.212 of the Companies Act 2014 to a dispute among shareholders.
key_quote: The oppression remedy under s.212 requires the petitioner to demonstrate that the company's affairs have been conducted in a manner oppressive to the petitioner.
full_summary: The petitioners brought proceedings under s.212 of the Companies Act 2014 alleging that the respondent had conducted the company's affairs in a manner oppressive to them. The High Court examined the evidence of oppressive conduct, including exclusion from management, failure to provide financial information, and diversion of corporate opportunities. The judgment provides a modern application of the oppression remedy and illustrates the court's approach to assessing oppressive conduct under the 2014 Act.

legal_principle: 2021 Irish authority on company law proceedings. The court addressed issues arising in the context of the company's corporate structure and governance.
key_quote: The court has jurisdiction to make appropriate orders to ensure that the company's affairs are conducted in accordance with the law.
full_summary: The High Court addressed company law issues arising in the context of a dispute concerning Oviedo Ltd. The court examined the corporate governance structure and the conduct of the company's affairs. The judgment addressed the court's jurisdiction to intervene in the management of a company's affairs where the law has been breached or where the rights of shareholders require protection.

legal_principle: 2024 Irish authority on company law disputes. The High Court addressed contemporary issues in corporate governance and shareholder rights.
key_quote: The court must ensure that corporate governance standards are maintained in the interests of all stakeholders.
full_summary: This 2024 High Court decision addressed company law issues arising from a dispute between shareholders and directors. The court examined contemporary standards of corporate governance and the rights of shareholders under the Companies Act 2014. The judgment provides recent guidance on the application of company law principles to modern corporate disputes.

case_name: McCool Controls and Engineering Ltd v. Honeywell Control Systems Ltd
legal_principle: 2024 Supreme Court authority on corporate relationships and contractual obligations between companies. The court addressed the liability of a parent company for the actions of its subsidiary.
key_quote: The separate legal personality of companies within a group must be respected unless the circumstances justify piercing the corporate veil.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the relationship between a parent company and its subsidiary in the context of contractual obligations. The court examined whether the parent company could be held liable for the contractual obligations of its subsidiary, applying the Salomon principle of separate legal personality. The judgment reaffirmed the principle that each company within a group has its own separate legal identity and the parent company is not automatically liable for the subsidiary's obligations. This is a recent Supreme Court authority on corporate group liability.

legal_principle: 2022 Irish authority on company law disputes. The court addressed issues of corporate management and the duties owed by directors in the context of a management company.
key_quote: Directors of management companies owe the same fiduciary duties as directors of any other company.
full_summary: The High Court addressed company law issues arising in the context of a management company dispute. The court examined the duties owed by directors of management companies, confirming that the same fiduciary duties apply to directors of management companies as to directors of any other company under the Companies Act 2014. The judgment addressed the particular governance challenges facing management companies and the rights of members to hold directors to account.

legal_principle: English authority on the powers of the liquidator in a winding up, including the power to disclaim onerous property and pursue claims against directors for misfeasance.
key_quote: The liquidator may pursue all available remedies for the benefit of the company's creditors.
full_summary: The case addressed the powers of the liquidator in the winding up of DPR Futures Ltd. The court examined the scope of the liquidator's powers under the Insolvency Act, including the power to investigate the affairs of the company, pursue claims against directors for misfeasance, and disclaim onerous contracts.
