The judgment addressed the practical aspects of liquidation and the liquidator's obligation to act in the best interests of the creditors as a whole.

legal_principle: Authority on the liquidator's power to set aside transactions at an undervalue and preferences. The court examined the criteria for setting aside transactions entered into by the company in the period before liquidation.
key_quote: A transaction at an undervalue entered into during the relevant period may be set aside by the liquidator.
full_summary: The liquidator sought to set aside transactions entered into by the company in the period before liquidation. The court examined the statutory provisions governing transactions at an undervalue and preferences, including the relevant time periods and the burden of proof. The judgment addressed the circumstances in which a liquidator may challenge pre-liquidation transactions and the defences available to the counterparty.

case_name: Inland Revenue Commissioners v. Blueslate Ltd
legal_principle: Authority on the priority of Revenue claims in a winding up and the treatment of tax debts as preferential or unsecured claims.
key_quote: The classification of Revenue claims in a winding up depends on the statutory framework governing preferential debts.
full_summary: The Inland Revenue claimed preferential status for certain tax debts in the winding up of Blueslate Ltd. The court examined the statutory framework for preferential debts and the circumstances in which Revenue claims are entitled to preferential treatment. The judgment addressed the ranking of different categories of Revenue claims and their priority in the distribution of the company's assets.

case_name: Sunwing Vacation Inc v. E-Clear (UK) plc
legal_principle: Authority on the rights of creditors in a winding up and the obligation of the liquidator to adjudicate on proofs of debt fairly and in accordance with the statutory framework.
key_quote: The liquidator must adjudicate on proofs of debt in accordance with the statutory rules and principles of fairness.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal considered an appeal relating to the adjudication of proofs of debt in the winding up of E-Clear (UK) plc. The court examined the liquidator's obligations in admitting or rejecting proofs of debt and the rights of creditors to challenge the liquidator's decisions. The judgment addressed the statutory framework governing the proof of debts and the principles of fairness applicable to the adjudication process.

case_name: MG Rover Dealer Properties Ltd v. Hunt
legal_principle: Authority on the liability of directors for misfeasance in the conduct of the company's affairs. The court examined the circumstances in which directors may be held personally liable for losses caused by their breach of duty.
key_quote: Directors who cause loss to the company through breach of duty may be held personally liable in misfeasance proceedings.
full_summary: The liquidator brought misfeasance proceedings against directors of a company within the MG Rover group. The court examined the directors' conduct and whether their actions constituted a breach of fiduciary duty or negligence causing loss to the company. The judgment addressed the standard of care expected of directors, the causation of loss, and the remedies available in misfeasance proceedings. The case provides guidance on the practical application of misfeasance provisions.

legal_principle: Modern English authority on the conduct of liquidations in complex corporate groups. The court addressed cross-border insolvency issues and the duties of liquidators in groups with international operations.
key_quote: The liquidator of a company within a complex group must consider the interests of all creditors, including those in other jurisdictions.
full_summary: The case arose from the liquidation of SCL Group Ltd, part of the corporate group associated with Cambridge Analytica. The High Court addressed complex issues arising in the liquidation of a company within an international corporate group, including cross-border claims, the duties of the liquidator in dealing with group companies, and the treatment of intercompany claims. The judgment provides modern guidance on the conduct of liquidations in complex corporate groups.

court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
legal_principle: Australian authority on the duties and liabilities of directors in the context of insolvent trading. Directors who allow a company to trade while insolvent may be personally liable for the company's debts.
key_quote: Directors have a duty to prevent insolvent trading, and failure to do so may result in personal liability.
full_summary: The case addressed the liability of directors for insolvent trading under Australian company law. The court examined the statutory provisions imposing personal liability on directors who allow a company to incur debts when the company is insolvent or becomes insolvent by incurring the debt. The judgment considered the defences available to directors, including reasonable grounds for expecting solvency, and the standard of care expected. The case is cited in comparative analysis with the Irish provisions on reckless and fraudulent trading.

court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
legal_principle: Australian authority on the disqualification of directors and the assessment of unfitness. The court considered the factors relevant to determining whether a director is unfit to manage a company.
key_quote: The assessment of unfitness considers the totality of the director's conduct and its impact on creditors and the public.
full_summary: The case addressed the disqualification of directors under Australian company law. The court examined the factors relevant to determining whether a director is unfit to manage a company, including the nature and extent of misconduct, the number of companies involved, and the impact on creditors. The judgment is cited in comparative analysis with Irish and English disqualification and restriction provisions.

case_name: Re William Lawrence (Globe Dyeworks) Pty Ltd (In Liquidation)
court: Supreme Court of New South Wales
legal_principle: Australian authority on the conduct of liquidations and the duties of liquidators in investigating and reporting on the conduct of directors.
key_quote: The liquidator has a duty to investigate the conduct of directors and to pursue claims where appropriate.
full_summary: The case addressed the duties of the liquidator in the context of the winding up of a manufacturing company. The court examined the liquidator's obligations to investigate the conduct of directors, to report on any misconduct, and to pursue claims against directors for the benefit of the company's creditors. The judgment provides comparative guidance on the conduct of liquidations.

case_name: IACS Pty Ltd v. Australian Flower Exports Pty Ltd (In Liquidation)
legal_principle: Australian authority on preferential payments and the rights of the liquidator to recover payments made in the period before liquidation that constitute unfair preferences.
key_quote: A payment that constitutes an unfair preference may be recovered by the liquidator for the benefit of the general body of creditors.
full_summary: The case addressed the recovery of preferential payments by the liquidator. The Federal Court examined the statutory provisions governing unfair preferences and the circumstances in which payments made by an insolvent company in the period before liquidation may be recovered. The court considered the defences available, including the running account defence and the good faith defence. The judgment is cited in comparative analysis with Irish provisions on fraudulent preference.

legal_principle: Irish Supreme Court authority on discovery obligations in litigation involving corporate entities. The court addressed the scope of discovery obligations owed by media companies and the balance with editorial independence.
key_quote: Discovery must be balanced against legitimate interests including editorial independence and proportionality.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered a discovery application in the context of litigation involving RTÉ. The court examined the scope of discovery obligations owed by corporate entities and the principles governing the balance between the right to discovery and competing interests such as editorial independence, journalistic sources, and proportionality. The judgment addressed the application of general discovery principles to the specific context of media companies.

case_name: Hartside Ltd v. Heineken Ireland Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of contractual obligations between companies and the application of company law principles to commercial disputes.
key_quote: Commercial contracts between companies must be construed in accordance with established principles of contract law, informed by company law.
full_summary: The High Court addressed a commercial dispute between corporate entities. The court examined the contractual relationship between the parties and the application of company law principles to the resolution of the dispute. The judgment addressed issues of corporate capacity, authority to contract, and the enforcement of commercial obligations between companies.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the interaction between company law and public law. The court addressed the standing of companies and shareholders to challenge public body decisions.
key_quote: A company, as a separate legal person, has standing to challenge decisions that affect its rights and interests.
full_summary: The case addressed the standing of a company to challenge a decision of Dublin City Council. The High Court examined the company's right, as a separate legal person, to bring proceedings challenging decisions of public bodies that affect the company's rights and interests. The judgment addressed the interaction between company law principles of separate legal personality and public law principles governing judicial review and standing.

legal_principle: Authority on the valuation of shares in the context of an unfair prejudice petition. The court examined the principles governing the valuation of minority shareholdings, including whether a minority discount should be applied.
key_quote: In a quasi-partnership company, shares should normally be valued without a minority discount on a buyout following unfair prejudice.
full_summary: The court considered the valuation of shares in the context of an unfair prejudice petition. The judgment addressed the principles governing share valuation, including the circumstances in which a minority discount should or should not be applied. The court held that in a quasi-partnership company, where the minority shareholder is being bought out as a result of unfairly prejudicial conduct, the shares should normally be valued on a pro rata basis without a minority discount. The case provides important guidance on share valuation in minority protection proceedings.

case_name: Comcast International Holdings Inc v. Minister for Public Enterprise
legal_principle: Irish Supreme Court authority on the relationship between company law and public law in the context of State-owned enterprises. The court examined the governance obligations of State entities.
key_quote: State-owned enterprises must comply with the same company law obligations as private companies.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered issues arising from the relationship between Comcast and State entities in the telecommunications sector. The court examined the governance obligations of State-owned enterprises under company law and the interaction between company law and the regulatory framework. The judgment addressed the duties of directors of State-owned companies and the accountability mechanisms applicable to such entities.

case_name: Quinn Supermarket Ltd v. Attorney General
legal_principle: The Supreme Court addressed the constitutional status of companies, holding that companies enjoy certain constitutional rights including the right to property and the right of access to the courts.
key_quote: A company, as a legal person, is entitled to certain constitutional protections.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered whether a company could invoke constitutional rights. The court held that companies are entitled to certain constitutional protections, including the right to property under Article 43 and the right of access to the courts. However, the court distinguished between rights that are personal to natural persons (such as the right to life and bodily integrity) and rights that can be enjoyed by artificial persons. The case is a leading Irish authority on the constitutional status of companies.

legal_principle: State-owned companies have the same legal personality and rights as private companies. The State cannot direct the affairs of a State company except through the mechanisms provided by company law.
key_quote: A State-owned company has its own separate legal personality and the State's control is exercised through the mechanisms of company law.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the legal status of Iarnród Éireann as a State-owned company. The court held that despite being wholly owned by the State, the company has its own separate legal personality and the State's control over the company must be exercised through the mechanisms provided by company law (shareholding, board appointments) rather than by direct ministerial direction. The judgment is an important authority on the legal position of State-owned companies in Irish law.

legal_principle: The identification doctrine applies to attribute the knowledge of a company's directing mind and will to the company. A company can be liable for the wilful misconduct of the person who represents its directing mind and will.
key_quote: The fault of the person who is the directing mind and will of the company is the fault of the company itself.
full_summary: A collision at sea between two vessels raised the question whether a company could be guilty of wilful misconduct through the acts of its alter ego. The Court of Appeal applied the identification doctrine from Lennard's Carrying Co v. Asiatic Petroleum, holding that the knowledge and intention of the person who represents the directing mind and will of the company is attributed to the company itself. The company was held liable for the wilful misconduct of its managing director, whose acts were treated as the acts of the company.

legal_principle: Irish authority on receivership and the rights of secured creditors. The court addressed the receiver's powers and duties in the management and sale of the company's assets.
key_quote: A receiver must exercise reasonable care in the management and sale of the company's assets.
full_summary: The case arose in the context of the receivership of Quigley Meats Ltd. The High Court examined the powers and duties of the receiver, including the duty to exercise reasonable care in managing and selling the company's assets, and the receiver's obligations to the company and its other creditors. The judgment addressed the practical aspects of receivership and the standard of care applicable to receivers.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the rule in Foss v. Harbottle and the circumstances in which a shareholder may bring proceedings on behalf of the company.
key_quote: A shareholder may only bring proceedings on behalf of the company where one of the recognised exceptions to the rule in Foss v. Harbottle applies.
full_summary: The plaintiff shareholder sought to bring proceedings on behalf of the company. The High Court examined the rule in Foss v. Harbottle and the recognised exceptions, including the fraud on the minority exception. The court considered whether the plaintiff had established the necessary conditions for a derivative action, including that the alleged wrongdoers were in control of the company and that the wrong amounted to a fraud on the minority.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of shareholders' agreements and the rights of shareholders to enforce contractual provisions that supplement the articles of association.
key_quote: A shareholders' agreement may create enforceable rights that supplement and are additional to the rights conferred by the articles.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the enforcement of a shareholders' agreement. The court held that shareholders' agreements create enforceable contractual rights between the parties that may supplement the provisions of the articles of association. The judgment addressed the relationship between shareholders' agreements and the articles and the remedies available for breach, including specific performance and damages.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the valuation of shares in the context of oppression proceedings. The court addressed the methodology for valuing shares and whether a minority discount should apply.
key_quote: The valuation methodology must be fair and appropriate to the circumstances, having regard to the nature of the company and the reason for the buyout.
full_summary: The High Court addressed the valuation of shares in the context of oppression proceedings under what is now s.212 of the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the appropriate valuation methodology, considering whether a minority discount should be applied. The judgment addressed the distinction between quasi-partnership companies (where no discount applies) and other companies, and the factors relevant to the choice of valuation date and methodology.

case_name: Curust Financial Services Ltd v. Loewe-Lack-Werk
legal_principle: Irish authority on the recognition and enforcement of foreign judgments against Irish companies. The court addressed the circumstances in which a foreign judgment may be enforced against an Irish company.
key_quote: A foreign judgment may be enforced against an Irish company where the company submitted to the jurisdiction of the foreign court.
full_summary: The Supreme Court considered the recognition and enforcement of a foreign judgment against an Irish company. The court examined the principles governing the enforcement of foreign judgments in Ireland, including the requirements of jurisdiction, submission, and the absence of fraud or public policy objections. The judgment addressed the position of companies in cross-border litigation and the recognition of foreign judgments.

legal_principle: Authority on the duty of directors to have regard to the interests of creditors when the company is insolvent or nearing insolvency.
key_quote: When a company is insolvent or nearing insolvency, directors must have regard to the interests of creditors.
full_summary: The case addressed the duties of directors when a company is insolvent or approaching insolvency. The court held that directors must have regard to the interests of creditors when the company is in this position, as it is the creditors' money that is at risk. The judgment contributed to the developing body of case law on the creditor duty, which was subsequently analysed definitively by the UK Supreme Court in BTI 2014 LLC v. Sequana.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the application to strike out proceedings as frivolous or vexatious in the context of company law litigation. The court examined the threshold for striking out and the right of access to the courts.
key_quote: Proceedings will only be struck out as frivolous or vexatious where they are clearly unsustainable.
full_summary: The defendant applied to strike out the plaintiff's proceedings as frivolous, vexatious, or disclosing no reasonable cause of action. The High Court examined the threshold for striking out proceedings in the context of company law litigation, balancing the right of access to the courts against the need to protect parties from abuse of process. The judgment applied the established principles governing strike-out applications.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the context of a cinema company. The court assessed the directors' conduct against the honest and responsible standard.
key_quote: The honest and responsible standard applies to all directors regardless of the nature of the company's business.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Dublin Cinema Group Ltd. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct against the honest and responsible standard under the restriction provisions, examining their management of the company, compliance with statutory obligations, and the circumstances leading to insolvency.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court addressed the application of restriction provisions to directors of property and investment companies.
key_quote: The restriction test applies equally to directors of all types of companies.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Albion Enterprises Ltd. The High Court applied the restriction provisions, examining the directors' conduct in the context of a property and investment company. The judgment addressed the particular considerations applicable to directors of companies operating in the property sector during the financial crisis.

legal_principle: Irish authority on receivership and the powers and duties of receivers in the management of companies in receivership. The court addressed the interaction between receivership and the ongoing management of the company.
key_quote: The receiver has a duty to manage the company's affairs prudently and to maximise the return for the secured creditor.
full_summary: The case addressed issues arising in the receivership of Goode Concrete. The High Court examined the powers and duties of the receiver in managing the company's business during receivership, the interaction between the receiver's powers and the directors' residual powers, and the duty to maximise the return for the debenture holder while having regard to the interests of the company. The judgment provides practical guidance on the conduct of receiverships.

legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on restriction proceedings under the Companies Act 2014. The court examined the application of s.819 to directors of a food manufacturing company.
key_quote: The restriction provisions serve to protect the public by ensuring that only fit persons participate in the management of companies.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Decobake Ltd under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court applied the honest and responsible test, examining the directors' compliance with statutory obligations, maintenance of books and records, and the conduct of the company's affairs generally. The judgment provides a modern application of the restriction provisions to the food manufacturing sector.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the relationship between companies and regulatory bodies. The court examined the powers of the Irish Stock Exchange to take disciplinary action against a member firm.
key_quote: The regulatory body must exercise its disciplinary powers in accordance with fair procedures and the principles of natural justice.
full_summary: The case addressed the relationship between Bloxham, a stockbroking firm, and the Irish Stock Exchange in the context of disciplinary proceedings. The High Court examined the regulatory body's powers and the procedural safeguards applicable to disciplinary actions against member firms. The judgment addressed the application of fair procedures to regulatory disciplinary proceedings and the rights of the company subject to such proceedings.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the context of a technology company that failed. The court examined the directors' conduct and whether they acted honestly and responsibly in managing a company during the dot-com bust.
key_quote: The court must consider the directors' conduct in the context of the commercial environment in which the company operated.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of 360 Atlantic, a telecommunications company that collapsed during the dot-com bust. The High Court examined the directors' conduct in the context of the challenging commercial environment. The court considered whether the directors had taken reasonable steps to address the company's financial difficulties, had maintained proper records, and had complied with statutory obligations. The judgment illustrates the application of the restriction test in the technology sector.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court clarified the factors to be considered in assessing whether directors acted responsibly, including compliance with statutory obligations and the maintenance of adequate financial controls.
key_quote: Responsible conduct requires directors to maintain adequate financial controls and comply with statutory obligations.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors under the Companies Acts. The High Court examined the conduct of the directors, focusing on compliance with statutory filing obligations, maintenance of proper books and records, and the adequacy of financial controls. The judgment clarified the specific factors that weigh for and against a finding of responsible conduct and is an important addition to the body of Irish restriction case law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors of a motor vehicle import company. The court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of the specific challenges of the motor trade.
key_quote: The restriction test must be applied having regard to the nature and circumstances of the company's business.
full_summary: Murphy J examined the conduct of the directors of a motor vehicle import company in liquidation. The court assessed whether the directors had acted honestly and responsibly, considering the particular features of the motor trade. The judgment contributes to the body of case law on the contextual application of the restriction test.

legal_principle: Court of Appeal authority on the restriction of directors. The court addressed the standard of review applicable to restriction decisions on appeal and the factors relevant to responsible conduct.
key_quote: The appellate court will only interfere with a restriction decision where the trial judge erred in principle or the decision was clearly wrong.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal considered an appeal from a High Court restriction order. The court examined the standard of review applicable to restriction decisions, holding that the appellate court will only interfere where the trial judge erred in principle or reached a decision that was clearly wrong on the facts. The judgment addressed the factors relevant to responsible conduct and the weight to be given to each factor.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings in the construction sector. The court assessed directors' conduct in the context of a civil engineering company that became insolvent.
key_quote: Directors of construction companies must exercise particular care in managing cash flow and contractual obligations.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a civil engineering company. The High Court examined the directors' management of the company's affairs, including cash flow management, compliance with contractual obligations, and dealings with creditors. The judgment illustrates the application of the restriction test in the construction sector.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties and liabilities of receivers. The court examined the receiver's obligations to the company and its creditors in the exercise of powers under a debenture.
key_quote: A receiver must act in good faith and with due regard to the interests of the company and its creditors.
full_summary: The case addressed the duties of a receiver appointed under a debenture over the assets of RMF (Ireland) Ltd. The High Court examined the receiver's obligations, including the duty to act in good faith, the duty to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable for the company's assets, and the duty to have regard to the interests of the company and its creditors. The judgment is an important Irish authority on receivership law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the aquaculture sector. The court assessed whether the directors of a seafood farming company had acted honestly and responsibly.
key_quote: The restriction test applies equally to all sectors, including aquaculture and farming.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a seafood farming company. The High Court examined the directors' conduct, including their management of the company's operations, compliance with regulatory requirements specific to the aquaculture sector, and maintenance of proper financial records. The judgment provides an application of the restriction test in a specialised sector.

case_name: Re RHS Energy Ltd; Diaz v. Palmer
legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on the restriction of directors under the Companies Act 2014 in the energy sector.
key_quote: Directors must demonstrate responsibility in the context of the regulatory requirements of the energy sector.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of an energy company under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of the energy sector, considering compliance with both Companies Act obligations and sector-specific regulatory requirements. The judgment provides a modern application of the restriction provisions.

legal_principle: Modern Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court applied the honest and responsible test under the Companies Act 2014.
key_quote: The statutory test requires directors to demonstrate both honesty and responsibility in the conduct of the company's affairs.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors under s.819 of the Companies Act 2014. The High Court applied the statutory test, examining the directors' conduct across all relevant dimensions. The judgment provides a recent application of the restriction provisions.

legal_principle: Early Irish authority on the separate legal personality of companies and the distinction between the company and its members. The company's rights and obligations are distinct from those of its shareholders.
key_quote: A company is a legal entity separate and distinct from its members.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the separate legal personality of a company in the context of industrial relations. The court confirmed that the company is a legal entity separate and distinct from its members and that the rights and obligations of the company are not the same as those of its shareholders or employees. The case applied the Salomon principle in the Irish context.

case_name: Allied Irish Coal Supplies Ltd v. Powell Duffryn International Fuels Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on corporate agency and the attribution of knowledge within corporate groups. The knowledge of an agent acting within the scope of authority is attributed to the company.
key_quote: Knowledge acquired by an agent within the scope of his authority is attributed to the principal company.
full_summary: The High Court addressed the principles of agency and the attribution of knowledge in the context of a dispute between companies in the coal supply business. The court examined when the knowledge of an agent is attributed to the company and the circumstances in which one company within a group may be treated as the agent of another. The judgment is an Irish authority on corporate agency principles.

case_name: O'Donnell v. Shanahan and Bank of Ireland
legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties of directors in the context of corporate transactions involving banks. Directors must act in the interests of the company and not in the interests of a controlling shareholder or lender.
key_quote: Directors must act in the interests of the company as a whole, not in the interests of any individual shareholder or creditor.
full_summary: The plaintiff challenged corporate transactions entered into by directors who were alleged to have acted in the interests of Bank of Ireland rather than the company. The High Court examined the fiduciary duties of directors and the circumstances in which directors may be in breach of duty by prioritising the interests of a lender over those of the company. The judgment is an Irish authority on directors' duties in the context of banking relationships.

case_name: Littlewoods Mail Order Stores Ltd v. IRC
legal_principle: Authority on the separate legal personality of subsidiaries within a group. The profits of a subsidiary belong to the subsidiary, not the parent, even where the parent controls the subsidiary.
key_quote: The subsidiary is a separate legal entity and its profits belong to it, not to the parent company.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal addressed the tax treatment of profits earned by a subsidiary company within a group. The Revenue argued that the profits should be attributed to the parent company. The court held that the subsidiary was a separate legal entity and its profits belonged to it, not to the parent. The mere fact that the parent controlled the subsidiary did not justify treating the subsidiary's profits as those of the parent. The case reinforces the Salomon principle in the group company context.

case_name: Glencar Exploration plc v. Mayo County Council (No 2)
legal_principle: The Supreme Court established the modern test for legitimate expectation in Irish public law. Relevant to company law in the context of reliance by companies on representations by public bodies.
key_quote: A company may rely on a legitimate expectation created by a clear and unambiguous representation of a public body.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the doctrine of legitimate expectation in the context of a mining company that relied on representations by a county council regarding mining permissions. The court established the modern framework for legitimate expectation, holding that a clear and unambiguous representation by a public body may create an enforceable expectation. The case is relevant to company law in the context of companies' reliance on public body representations and the remedies available.

legal_principle: Authority on fair procedures in the context of professional and regulatory proceedings that may affect a company or its officers. Constitutional fair procedures apply to decisions that affect economic interests.
key_quote: Fair procedures must be observed in decisions that affect the economic interests of individuals and companies.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the requirements of fair procedures in the context of regulatory proceedings. The court held that constitutional fair procedures must be observed in decisions that affect the economic interests of individuals and companies, including the right to be heard, the right to know the case against one, and the right to an impartial decision-maker. The judgment has implications for company law proceedings involving regulatory bodies.

case_name: Merck Sharp and Dohme Corporation v. Clonmel Healthcare Ltd
legal_principle: Supreme Court authority on discovery in the context of corporate litigation. The court addressed proportionality and the scope of discovery obligations in complex commercial disputes between companies.
key_quote: Discovery must be proportionate to the issues in dispute and must not impose an undue burden on the party making discovery.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed discovery in complex commercial litigation between pharmaceutical companies. The court examined the principles of proportionality in discovery, holding that the scope of discovery must be proportionate to the issues in the case and the burden on the discovering party. The judgment has significance for company law litigation involving large corporate entities.

case_name: Irish Press plc v. Ingersoll Irish Publications Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on minority shareholder oppression in the context of a joint venture between a major Irish newspaper group and a US media company. The court examined the obligations of joint venture partners.
key_quote: In a joint venture company, each partner has a legitimate expectation that the venture will be conducted in good faith and for the purposes agreed.
full_summary: Irish Press brought oppression proceedings against its joint venture partner, Ingersoll Irish Publications. The High Court examined the conduct of the joint venture and whether the respondent had conducted the company's affairs in a manner oppressive to the petitioner. The judgment addressed the application of the oppression remedy to joint venture companies and the legitimate expectations of joint venture partners.

case_name: Re Rahill Property Co; Conroy v. Corneill
legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors of a property company. The court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of the property market downturn.
key_quote: The court must assess responsibility in the context of the economic conditions prevailing during the period under review.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a property company that became insolvent following the property market collapse. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of the prevailing economic conditions, considering whether the directors had taken reasonable steps to manage the company through the downturn.

legal_principle: Irish authority on examinership. The court examined the criteria for the appointment of an examiner to a manufacturing company and the prospects of survival as a going concern.
key_quote: The survival test requires credible evidence that the company has a reasonable prospect of survival if given the protection of the court.
full_summary: The High Court considered an application for the appointment of an examiner to Cavan Crystal Group, a well-known Irish crystal manufacturing company. The court examined whether the company met the statutory criteria for examinership, including the survival test. The judgment addressed the evidence required to satisfy the court that there is a reasonable prospect of the company surviving as a going concern.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors. The court examined the duty of directors to cooperate with the liquidator and the consequences of failure to do so.
key_quote: Failure to cooperate with the liquidator is a significant factor weighing against a finding of responsible conduct.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors who had failed to cooperate with the liquidation process. The High Court held that cooperation with the liquidator is a significant factor in the assessment of responsible conduct. Directors who fail to provide books, records, and information to the liquidator, or who obstruct the liquidation process, are unlikely to satisfy the honest and responsible test.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the duties of directors and the misfeasance remedy. The court examined the circumstances in which a liquidator may pursue a claim for misfeasance against directors.
key_quote: A director who causes loss to the company through breach of duty may be held liable in misfeasance proceedings.
full_summary: The liquidator brought misfeasance proceedings against the directors. The High Court examined the elements of misfeasance in the winding up context, including the requirement to prove a breach of fiduciary duty or duty of care, causation, and loss. The judgment provides guidance on the practical application of the misfeasance remedy.

case_name: Secretary of State for Trade v. Tjolle
legal_principle: The court examined the standard of conduct expected of non-executive directors in the context of disqualification proceedings. Non-executive directors have a duty to inform themselves about the company's affairs and cannot rely on ignorance as a defence.
key_quote: A non-executive director is not entitled to close his eyes to what is going on around him.
full_summary: The Secretary of State sought the disqualification of directors, including non-executive directors. The court held that non-executive directors are under a duty to inform themselves about the company's affairs and cannot rely on their non-executive status as a defence to allegations of misconduct. While the standard may be somewhat lower than for executive directors, non-executive directors cannot simply abdicate responsibility.

legal_principle: The Court of Appeal established that a minority shareholder bringing a derivative action on behalf of the company is entitled to an indemnity for costs from the company. The shareholder acts as a representative of the company's interests.
key_quote: A minority shareholder who brings a derivative action in good faith is entitled to be indemnified by the company for his costs.
full_summary: Wallersteiner, a dominant shareholder, was alleged to have misused the company's funds. Moir, a minority shareholder, brought a derivative action. The Court of Appeal held that a minority shareholder who brings a derivative action in good faith for the benefit of the company is entitled to an indemnity from the company for the costs of the proceedings. This is necessary to ensure that the cost of litigation does not deter minority shareholders from bringing meritorious derivative claims.

legal_principle: A service agreement between a director and a holding company may be construed as requiring the director to serve the group, including its subsidiaries. The group structure does not prevent a director owing duties to subsidiary companies.
key_quote: A director appointed to the group may owe duties to subsidiaries within the group, depending on the terms of his appointment.
full_summary: Caddies was appointed as managing director under a service agreement with the holding company but was required to work for a subsidiary. When dismissed, the question was whether the holding company was liable under the service agreement. The House of Lords held that the service agreement could properly be construed as requiring Caddies to serve the group, including its subsidiaries. The case is relevant to the position of directors in group companies.

legal_principle: Fraud unravels all. A document obtained by fraud is vitiated, even if the fraud was committed against a third party. The principle applies in the corporate context to transactions obtained through fraudulent misrepresentation.
key_quote: No court in this land will allow a person to keep an advantage which he has obtained by fraud.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal applied the principle that fraud unravels all. The case concerned a document obtained through fraudulent misrepresentation. Denning LJ held that no court will allow a person to retain an advantage obtained through fraud. The principle has been applied in the corporate context to transactions, documents, and corporate acts that are vitiated by fraud.

legal_principle: Directors who purchase property and then sell it to the company at a profit are in breach of their fiduciary duty unless the company consents after full disclosure. A promoter's secret profit must be disgorged.
key_quote: A promoter who sells property to the company must disclose the profit he makes and obtain the company's informed consent.
full_summary: Directors who were also promoters purchased mining property and sold it to the company at a significant profit without disclosing their interest. The Court of Appeal held that the directors/promoters were in breach of their fiduciary duty and were liable to disgorge the secret profit. The case illustrates the strict application of the no-profit rule to promoters and directors who deal with the company's property.

case_name: Re Claridges Patent Asphalt Co Ltd
legal_principle: Authority on the distinction between a return of capital and a payment of income. A company may not return capital to shareholders except through a proper reduction of capital.
key_quote: A company may not return capital to shareholders in the guise of a dividend or other distribution.
full_summary: The case addressed the distinction between a return of capital and a payment of income to shareholders. The court held that a company may not return capital to shareholders except through a proper reduction of capital authorised by the court. Payments to shareholders that are in substance a return of capital, disguised as income distributions, are unlawful. The case is an authority on the capital maintenance doctrine.

case_name: Re E-Host Europe Ltd; Coyle v. O'Brien
legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the technology sector. The court assessed the conduct of directors of an internet hosting company.
key_quote: The restriction test is applied consistently across all sectors, including the technology sector.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of an internet hosting company. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct, including their management of the technology business, compliance with statutory obligations, and the circumstances leading to insolvency. The judgment provides an application of the restriction test to the technology sector.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the winding up of foreign companies with assets in Ireland. The court has jurisdiction to wind up a foreign company where it has a sufficient connection with Ireland.
key_quote: The court has jurisdiction to wind up a foreign company where there is a sufficient connection with the jurisdiction.
full_summary: The High Court addressed the jurisdiction to wind up a foreign company. The court held that it has jurisdiction to wind up a company incorporated outside Ireland where there is a sufficient connection with the jurisdiction, such as the presence of assets, creditors, or business operations in Ireland. The judgment addresses the jurisdictional requirements for the winding up of foreign companies and is relevant to cross-border insolvency.

case_name: Wee Care Ltd v. Companies Registration Office
legal_principle: Irish authority on the powers of the Companies Registration Office and the process for striking off and restoring companies to the register.
key_quote: A company that has been struck off may apply to the court for restoration to the register.
full_summary: The case addressed the process of striking off companies from the register and the circumstances in which a company may be restored. The High Court examined the statutory powers of the CRO to strike off companies for failure to file annual returns and the procedure for applying to the court for restoration. The judgment provides guidance on the practical aspects of the strike-off and restoration process.

legal_principle: Irish authority on examinership in the healthcare sector. The court examined the criteria for appointing an examiner and the prospects of survival for a medical services company.
key_quote: The survival test requires credible evidence of a reasonable prospect of survival as a going concern.
full_summary: The High Court considered an application for the appointment of an examiner to a healthcare company. The court examined whether the company met the statutory criteria for examinership, including the survival test. The judgment addressed the particular considerations applicable to companies in the healthcare sector.

case_name: Rex Petfoods Ltd v. Lamb Brothers (Dublin) Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on corporate capacity and the enforceability of contracts entered into by companies. The court examined whether the company had capacity to enter the contract.
key_quote: A contract entered into within the company's capacity is binding on the company.
full_summary: The case addressed the corporate capacity of the defendant company and whether the contract in question was within the company's objects. The High Court examined the principles governing corporate capacity, including the construction of the objects clause and the ultra vires doctrine. The judgment addressed the consequences of ultra vires acts and the protections available to third parties dealing with the company.

legal_principle: Authority on the relationship between the company's property and the interests of third parties. The case addressed constructive trusts in the context of company property.
key_quote: A constructive trust may arise over property held by a company where it would be unconscionable for the company to assert its legal title.
full_summary: The case addressed the circumstances in which a constructive trust may be imposed on property held by a company. The court held that where it would be unconscionable for the company to assert its legal title to property, a constructive trust may arise in favour of a third party. The judgment is relevant to the treatment of company property in insolvency.

legal_principle: Leading Irish authority on dismissal of proceedings for delay. While a general civil procedure case, it has significant implications for company law proceedings that are subject to delay.
key_quote: The court has inherent jurisdiction to dismiss proceedings for want of prosecution where the delay is inordinate and inexcusable and the balance of justice favours dismissal.
full_summary: The Supreme Court established the test for dismissal of proceedings for delay. The court held that proceedings may be dismissed where the delay is inordinate and inexcusable and the balance of justice favours dismissal. This test has been applied extensively in company law proceedings, including oppression petitions, misfeasance claims, and restriction applications.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the delay in bringing proceedings in the company law context. The court examined the effect of delay on the right to bring oppression or similar proceedings.
key_quote: Delay in bringing proceedings may bar a party from relief where the delay has caused prejudice.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the effect of delay on company law proceedings. The court examined the principles governing delay and its consequences, holding that excessive delay in bringing proceedings may bar a party from obtaining relief where the delay has caused prejudice to the respondent. The judgment has implications for the timely prosecution of company law claims.

legal_principle: Follow-on decision addressing further procedural aspects of delay in company law proceedings.
key_quote: The court must balance the right to bring proceedings against the prejudice caused by delay.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed further procedural issues arising from delay in the same company law proceedings. The court examined the balance between the plaintiff's right to bring proceedings and the prejudice caused to the defendant by the delay. The judgment provides additional guidance on the management of delay in company law litigation.

case_name: McBrearty v. North Western Health Board
legal_principle: Supreme Court authority on fair procedures and delay. While a public law case, the principles regarding fair procedures and delay apply to company law proceedings involving regulatory and public bodies.
key_quote: The right to fair procedures requires that proceedings are conducted without unreasonable delay.
full_summary: The Supreme Court addressed the impact of delay on fair procedures in the context of proceedings involving a public body. The court held that unreasonable delay may infringe the right to fair procedures and that the court must balance the right to bring proceedings against the prejudice caused by delay. The principles apply to company law proceedings involving regulatory bodies.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the just and equitable winding up ground and its relationship with oppression proceedings. The court examined the circumstances in which a winding up petition may be brought on the just and equitable ground.
key_quote: The just and equitable ground for winding up may be invoked where the petitioner has no other adequate remedy.
full_summary: The petitioner sought the winding up of the company on the just and equitable ground. The High Court examined the relationship between the just and equitable winding up ground and the oppression remedy under s.212, and the circumstances in which winding up is more appropriate than an oppression order. The judgment addressed the overlap between these remedies and the factors the court considers in choosing between them.

legal_principle: Early authority on the liability of directors for misapplication of company funds. Directors who apply company funds for purposes not authorised by the company's constitution are liable to account.
key_quote: Directors who misapply the company's funds are liable to account to the company for the amounts misapplied.
full_summary: The case addressed the liability of directors who had misapplied the company's funds. The Court of Chancery held that directors who apply company funds for purposes not authorised by the company's constitution are liable to account to the company for the amounts misapplied. The case illustrates the early development of the duty of directors to apply company funds only for proper purposes.

legal_principle: Authority on the valuation of shares in the context of an unfair prejudice petition. The court considered the appropriate valuation methodology and the date of valuation.
key_quote: The date of valuation in unfair prejudice proceedings is a matter of discretion for the court, determined by the circumstances of the case.
full_summary: The court addressed the valuation of shares in the context of an unfair prejudice petition. The judgment considered the appropriate valuation methodology, the date at which shares should be valued, and whether a minority discount should be applied. The court held that the date of valuation is a matter of discretion and should be determined by the circumstances of the case.

case_name: Munton Brothers Ltd v. Secretary of State
legal_principle: Authority on the disqualification of directors for persistent default in filing returns. Persistent failure to comply with filing obligations may result in disqualification.
key_quote: Persistent default in filing obligations is a ground for disqualification.
full_summary: The Secretary of State applied for the disqualification of directors on the grounds of persistent default in filing annual returns and other statutory documents. The court held that persistent failure to comply with filing obligations is a ground for disqualification and that the court must consider the nature and extent of the default.

case_name: Lindgren v. L P Estates Co Ltd
legal_principle: Authority on the enforcement of pre-emption rights in the articles of association. Pre-emption rights are enforceable as part of the statutory contract between the company and its members.
key_quote: Pre-emption rights in the articles are enforceable as part of the statutory contract.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal addressed the enforcement of pre-emption rights contained in the articles of association. The court held that pre-emption rights are part of the statutory contract between the company and its members and are enforceable by any member. The judgment addressed the procedure for exercising pre-emption rights and the consequences of a failure to offer shares in accordance with the articles.

case_name: Re Architectural and Industrial Holdings Ltd
legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of a holding company structure.
key_quote: Directors of holding companies must exercise oversight of subsidiary operations.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a holding company. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct in the context of a holding company structure, considering whether the directors had exercised appropriate oversight of subsidiary operations. The judgment addresses the duties of directors at the holding company level.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors in the digital media sector. The court applied the honest and responsible test to directors of a technology company.
key_quote: The honest and responsible test applies uniformly across all business sectors.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a digital media company. The High Court applied the honest and responsible test, examining the directors' management of the technology business, compliance with statutory obligations, and the circumstances leading to insolvency.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction of directors in the hospitality sector. The court assessed the directors' conduct in managing a café/bar business.
key_quote: Directors of hospitality businesses must manage finances prudently and comply with tax and employment obligations.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a sports café business. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct, including management of finances, compliance with tax obligations, employment law compliance, and the maintenance of proper books and records.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction of directors. The court applied the honest and responsible test to directors of a company in liquidation.
key_quote: Directors must demonstrate honesty and responsibility in relation to the conduct of the company's affairs.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of Shellware Ltd. The High Court applied the statutory test, examining the directors' conduct across all relevant dimensions including compliance with statutory obligations, maintenance of proper records, and dealings with creditors.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court assessed the directors' conduct in a manufacturing company context.
key_quote: All statutory obligations must be complied with for directors to demonstrate responsible conduct.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a manufacturing company. The High Court examined the directors' compliance with statutory obligations and their management of the company's affairs. The judgment contributes to the body of restriction case law.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction of directors of an investment company. The court assessed the directors' management of the company's investment portfolio.
key_quote: Directors of investment companies must exercise reasonable care in the management of the company's investments.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of an investment company. The High Court assessed the directors' management of the company's investment portfolio and their compliance with statutory obligations. The judgment addresses the application of the restriction test to investment companies.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors of a logistics company. The court assessed compliance with employment, tax, and company law obligations.
key_quote: Directors must comply with all relevant statutory frameworks including employment law, tax law, and company law.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a logistics company. The High Court assessed the directors' compliance with multiple statutory frameworks and their management of the company. The judgment illustrates the breadth of considerations in restriction proceedings.

legal_principle: Foundational authority on privity of contract with implications for company law. Only a party to a contract may enforce it; a company that is not a party to a contract between its shareholders cannot enforce it and vice versa.
key_quote: Only a person who is a party to a contract can sue upon it.
full_summary: The House of Lords addressed the doctrine of privity of contract. Viscount Haldane LC held that only a person who is a party to a contract can sue upon it. The case has significant implications for company law, particularly in relation to the distinction between the statutory contract created by the articles (which binds the company and its members) and ordinary contracts between members or between the company and third parties.

legal_principle: The general meeting cannot usurp the powers of the board of directors where the articles vest management powers in the board. The shareholders' remedy is to change the directors or amend the articles.
key_quote: Where the articles vest powers of management in the directors, the general meeting cannot interfere with those powers.
full_summary: The Privy Council addressed the division of powers between the board of directors and the general meeting. The court held that where the articles vest powers of management in the board, the general meeting cannot override the board's exercise of those powers. The shareholders' remedy, if dissatisfied with the board's decisions, is to remove the directors by resolution or to amend the articles. This decision, alongside Breckland v. London and Suffolk Properties, establishes the principle of board supremacy in management matters.

legal_principle: Directors' powers must be exercised at a properly constituted board meeting unless the articles provide otherwise. A decision by directors acting individually or informally may not bind the company.
key_quote: The power of management vested in directors can only be exercised at a meeting of the board of directors properly convened.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal addressed the requirement that directors exercise their powers collectively at properly constituted board meetings. The court held that unless the articles provide otherwise, directors must act as a board at a properly convened meeting. Decisions made by individual directors or by directors acting informally outside of a board meeting do not bind the company. The case establishes the requirement of collective decision-making by the board.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the standing of shareholders in the context of company disputes and the rule in Foss v. Harbottle. The court examined the circumstances in which a shareholder may bring proceedings in its own name rather than derivatively.
key_quote: A shareholder may only bring proceedings in its own name where the wrong is a wrong to the shareholder personally, not a wrong to the company.
full_summary: The High Court examined the rule in Foss v. Harbottle in the context of proceedings brought by Quinn Investments. The court considered whether the wrongs alleged were wrongs to the company (requiring a derivative action) or wrongs to the shareholder personally (permitting a personal action). The judgment addressed the distinction between personal and derivative claims in company litigation.

case_name: Barclay-Watt v. Alpha Airports Group plc
legal_principle: 2022 Irish authority on shareholder disputes and the application of company law principles to modern corporate governance issues.
key_quote: The court must ensure that company law principles are applied to protect the rights of all stakeholders.
full_summary: The High Court addressed company law issues arising in the context of a shareholder dispute. The court examined the application of company law principles to modern corporate governance questions, including the rights of shareholders and the duties of directors.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the enforcement of shareholders' agreements and the duties of directors in the context of shareholder disputes.
key_quote: A shareholders' agreement creates enforceable contractual obligations between the parties.
full_summary: The case addressed the enforcement of a shareholders' agreement in the context of a dispute between shareholders. The High Court examined the contractual obligations created by the agreement and the remedies available for breach. The judgment addressed the interaction between shareholders' agreements and the duties of directors.

legal_principle: Irish authority on restriction proceedings. The court assessed the directors' conduct in the hospitality/restaurant sector.
key_quote: The honest and responsible test applies equally to directors of hospitality businesses.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of a hospitality company. The High Court assessed the directors' conduct, including their management of the restaurant business, compliance with tax obligations, and maintenance of proper records.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the restriction of directors of an engineering company. The court assessed compliance with statutory obligations.
key_quote: Compliance with statutory obligations is a fundamental requirement for responsible conduct.
full_summary: The liquidator applied for the restriction of directors of an engineering company. The High Court assessed the directors' compliance with statutory obligations and their management of the engineering business.

legal_principle: Irish authority on the rights of members of companies limited by guarantee. The court examined the relationship between the articles of association and the rights of members.
key_quote: Members of companies limited by guarantee have rights determined by the articles of association.
full_summary: The case addressed the rights of members of the Medical Defence Union, a company limited by guarantee. The High Court examined the scope of members' rights as defined by the articles of association and the statutory contract. The judgment addressed the particular features of companies limited by guarantee and the rights of their members.

case_name: 3V Transaction Services Ltd v. Registrar of Companies
legal_principle: Irish authority on the powers of the Registrar of Companies and the process for challenging decisions of the CRO.
key_quote: Decisions of the Registrar of Companies may be challenged by way of application to the court.
full_summary: The case addressed a challenge to a decision of the Registrar of Companies. The High Court examined the statutory powers of the Registrar and the process for challenging CRO decisions. The judgment addressed the grounds on which a CRO decision may be overturned.

case_name: Air France Aircraft Leasing Ltd v. Registrar of Companies
legal_principle: Irish authority on the restoration of companies to the register and the CRO process. The court examined the circumstances in which restoration may be ordered.
key_quote: The court may order the restoration of a company to the register where it is just and equitable to do so.
full_summary: The case addressed an application for the restoration of a company to the register following strike-off. The High Court examined the statutory framework for restoration, including the time limits and the factors the court considers, such as whether the company has assets, whether third parties would be prejudiced, and whether it is just and equitable to restore the company.

legal_principle: The High Court refused to strike out proceedings seeking to pierce the corporate veil. The court applied the Prest v. Petrodel framework to Irish law, holding there were arguable grounds for treating two companies as a single entity where the principal shareholder interposed one to evade contractual liability.
key_quote: There are arguable grounds for treating the two corporate defendants as a single entity where one was interposed to evade contractual liability.
full_summary: In a construction contract dispute, the plaintiffs sought to pierce the corporate veil between two corporate defendants controlled by the same individual. The High Court refused the application to strike out, holding that the plaintiffs had raised arguable grounds for veil-piercing under the Prest v. Petrodel framework. The court applied both the concealment principle and the evasion principle, finding that there was an arguable case that the second company was interposed to evade the contractual obligations of the first. This is a significant 2023 Irish authority on the application of the modern veil-piercing framework.

case_name: Tesco Ireland Ltd v. Stateline Transport Ltd
legal_principle: Directors may be personally liable for contempt where a company fails to comply with a court order. Section 53 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a mechanism for holding corporate officers personally responsible for ensuring corporate compliance.
key_quote: Separate legal personality will not protect corporate officers who fail to ensure compliance with court orders.
full_summary: Tesco Ireland brought proceedings against Stateline Transport for breach of a court order. Simons J held that directors may face personal liability for contempt under section 53 of the Companies Act 2014 where a company fails to comply with a court order and the directors failed to take reasonable steps to ensure compliance. The judgment confirms that separate legal personality does not shield directors from the consequences of deliberate corporate non-compliance.

case_name: Downtul Ltd (In Liquidation) v. Companies Act
legal_principle: Directors of a company within a group structure must separately consider and weigh the interests of each company as an independent entity. Failure to do so demonstrates a lack of responsible conduct. Five-year restriction orders were imposed.
key_quote: There was a fundamental failure to understand or appreciate the need to separately consider and weigh the interests of Downtul as a separate legal entity.
full_summary: Cahill J imposed five-year restriction orders on two directors who were directors of over 130 companies each. The company held a lease for a Starbucks outlet but had no revenue, while a related company operated the premises without any enforceable rent obligation. The court found the directors acted honestly but not responsibly, citing: no board minutes after 2017, no bank account, abridged financial statements omitting material items, and a fundamental failure to consider the interests of the company as a standalone entity within the group. The court also excluded an expert witness who had not disclosed accepting twelve liquidator appointments from the same directors. This is a landmark 2025 authority on directors' duties in group structures.

legal_principle: The Supreme Court held that ill-health does not ground an entitlement to have civil proceedings for breach of directors' duties dismissed. The case concerns approximately EUR 290 million in losses from loans issued between 2006-2009.
key_quote: Ill-health does not, in itself, ground an entitlement to have civil proceedings dismissed.
full_summary: The Supreme Court refused to dismiss a negligence and breach of directors' duty claim against former Irish Nationwide Building Society CEO Michael Fingleton on grounds of his incapacity following a stroke. Woulfe J held that the claim, alleging gross negligence and breach of the duty to exercise due skill, care and diligence in authorising five loans totalling approximately EUR 290 million, should proceed. The judgment is a significant 2024 Supreme Court authority on directors' duty of care and the procedural rights of parties in directors' duty litigation.

legal_principle: The High Court imposed personal liability of USD 30.8 million on directors for fraudulent and reckless trading. This was the first case where a receiver (rather than liquidator) brought a reckless/fraudulent trading application. The company had consistently overstated intangible assets and was insolvent since at least March 2017.
key_quote: The directors were knowingly a party to the carrying on of the business in a reckless manner with intent to defraud creditors.
full_summary: The receiver of DQ Entertainment brought proceedings against three directors for fraudulent and reckless trading under the Companies Act. The High Court imposed personal liability of USD 30.8 million. The company had consistently overstated its intangible assets and had been insolvent since at least March 2017. Significantly, this was the first case where a receiver (rather than a liquidator) brought such an application, requiring proof that the company was unable to pay its debts as they fell due. The judgment is a landmark authority on the practical application of the reckless and fraudulent trading provisions and the standing of receivers to bring such claims.

case_name: Re Glenman Corporation Ltd; Conneally v. Conneally
legal_principle: The High Court refused an application for an in camera hearing of s.212 oppression proceedings. The constitutional principle of open justice under Article 34.1 prevails. Something 'extraordinary, bordering on public policy reasons' is required before in camera orders will be granted in s.212 proceedings.
key_quote: If mere disclosure of private disputes between directors were sufficient, almost all s.212 proceedings would be heard behind closed doors.
full_summary: In oppression proceedings between two brothers who were shareholders and directors (holding 60% and 40% respectively), Cregan J refused an application for the proceedings to be heard in camera. The court held that the constitutional principle that justice must be administered in public prevails, and that the mere fact that the proceedings involve private shareholder disputes is not sufficient to justify a departure from open justice. The judgment provides important guidance on the procedural aspects of s.212 oppression proceedings.

case_name: In the Matter of GTLK Europe DAC (In Liquidation)
legal_principle: In one of the largest liquidations in Irish history, the court declared void pledge agreements over 37 aircraft valued at over USD 2 billion. The court granted the first-ever written anti-suit injunction in Irish law, restraining a Russian sanctioned entity from advancing parallel proceedings.
key_quote: A person who submits claims in an Irish liquidation thereby submits to the court's jurisdiction.
full_summary: Mulcahy J addressed the liquidation of GTLK Europe DAC, one of the largest in Irish history. The court declared void pledge agreements entered into by JSC (a Russian state-owned sanctioned entity) over 37 aircraft valued at over USD 2 billion. The court also granted the first-ever written anti-suit injunction in Irish law, restraining JSC from advancing parallel Russian proceedings designed to undermine the Irish liquidation. The judgment established that a person who submits claims in an Irish liquidation thereby submits to the court's jurisdiction. This is a landmark authority on cross-border insolvency, sanctions, and the protection of Irish liquidation proceedings.

case_name: In the Matter of Mac Interiors Ltd (Appointment)
legal_principle: The first case where the Irish High Court appointed an examiner to a company incorporated outside the State (and outside the EU). Jurisdiction was established on the basis that the company's centre of main interests (COMI) was in Ireland.
key_quote: The court has jurisdiction to appoint an examiner to a foreign company where its centre of main interests is in Ireland.
full_summary: Quinn J appointed an examiner to Mac Interiors, a company incorporated in Northern Ireland but with its centre of main interests in the Republic of Ireland. This was the first time the Irish High Court had appointed an examiner to a company incorporated outside the State. The court confirmed jurisdiction under section 509 of the Companies Act 2014, significantly extending the territorial reach of the Irish examinership regime. The decision is a landmark authority on the jurisdiction of Irish courts in cross-border examinership.

case_name: In the Matter of Mac Interiors Ltd (Creditor Classification)
legal_principle: The court refused to confirm the examiner's scheme of arrangement due to material irregularity in creditor class composition. The test for class composition in Part 9 schemes also applies to Part 10 examinership schemes.
key_quote: The test for creditor class composition in schemes of arrangement applies equally to examinership schemes.
full_summary: Quinn J refused to confirm the scheme of arrangement proposed by the examiner for Mac Interiors due to a material irregularity in the composition of creditor classes. The court held that the test for class composition in Part 9 schemes of arrangement also applies to examinership schemes under Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014. This was the most detailed judicial analysis of creditor class composition since the inception of the Irish examinership regime.

case_name: Custom House Capital Ltd (In Liquidation) v. Companies Acts
legal_principle: The court addressed the treatment of unclaimed funds in a long-running liquidation (commenced 2011, EUR 61 million misappropriated), ordering undistributed funds to be lodged under s.623(1) of the Companies Act 2014.
key_quote: It would be intolerable to keep the liquidation open indefinitely.
full_summary: O'Moore J addressed the practical conclusion of a complex liquidation involving Custom House Capital Ltd, where EUR 61 million had been misappropriated. The liquidation had been ongoing since 2011. The court ordered that undistributed funds be lodged with the Minister for Finance under section 623(1) of the Companies Act 2014. The judgment provides guidance on the practical conclusion of complex liquidations involving unclaimed client funds and the court's role in bringing long-running liquidations to an end.

case_name: Barclays Bank Ireland PLC v. Companies Act 2014
legal_principle: The court approved a scheme of arrangement for reconstruction, confirming that a scheme can be one step in a pre-planned multi-step transaction. Irish solvent schemes of arrangement may be more flexible than their English equivalents.
key_quote: A scheme of arrangement can be one step in a pre-planned multi-step transaction including a cross-border merger.
full_summary: Quinn J approved a scheme of arrangement for the reconstruction of Barclays Bank Ireland PLC under s.455 of the Companies Act 2014. The court confirmed that a scheme of arrangement can be one step in a pre-planned multi-step transaction (followed by a cross-border merger), that a members-only scheme is permissible where creditor rights are not altered, and that Irish solvent schemes may be more flexible than English equivalents. The judgment provides important guidance on the use of schemes of arrangement for corporate restructuring.

case_name: In the Matter of CityJet DAC
legal_principle: The court approved a rescue plan applying the 'best-interest-of-creditors' test under s.541(3A) of the Companies Act 2014 as amended by the Preventive Restructuring Directive. The examiner's assessment that liquidation would yield only 0.99% versus the 2% offered by the scheme was upheld.
key_quote: The best-interest-of-creditors test requires comparison between the scheme outcome and the likely outcome in liquidation.
full_summary: Quinn J approved the rescue plan for CityJet (its third examinership). The case provided the first detailed analysis of the 'best-interest-of-creditors' test under section 541(3A) of the Companies Act 2014 (as amended by the Preventive Restructuring Directive). The examiner assessed that a winding up would provide only a 0.99% dividend versus the 2% offered by the scheme. The court rejected the shareholder entities' challenge (which argued the liquidation dividend would be 3.53%) and confirmed the scheme. The judgment is a landmark authority on the application of the Preventive Restructuring Directive in Ireland.

case_name: Bio Marine Ingredients Ireland Ltd v. Companies Act 2014
legal_principle: The court granted creditor protection to a small company under the SCARP (Small Company Administrative Rescue Process) under Part 10A of the Companies Act 2014. This illustrates the operation of the new cost-effective alternative to formal examinership.
key_quote: Part 10A provides a cost-effective rescue mechanism for small and micro companies.
full_summary: Sanfey J granted creditor protection to a small company that had entered the Small Company Administrative Rescue Process (SCARP) under Part 10A of the Companies Act 2014, introduced by the Companies (Rescue Process for Small and Micro Companies) Act 2021. The judgment illustrates how Part 10A operates as a cost-effective alternative to formal examinership for small and micro companies, while still permitting court applications for creditor protection where necessary.

case_name: In the Matter of Tower Trade Finance (Ireland) Ltd
legal_principle: The Court of Appeal held that directors' imprecise and unenforceable funding representations during examinership did not justify refusing all remuneration to the examiner. Directors are warned that misleading funding commitments during examinership can have serious consequences.
key_quote: Misleading or unenforceable funding commitments by directors during examinership can have serious consequences.
full_summary: The Court of Appeal allowed the examiner's appeal against the High Court's refusal to award costs and remuneration. The court held that the directors' imprecise and unenforceable representations about providing funding to the company during examinership did not amount to a deliberate or grave breach of duty by the examiner justifying a refusal of all remuneration. The judgment warns directors that misleading or unenforceable funding commitments during examinership can have serious consequences.

case_name: Claddagh Jewellers Ltd v. Companies Act 2014 (Fried v. Fried)
legal_principle: Irish authority on oppression proceedings in the context of a family business. The case involved allegations of oppressive conduct including unlawful transfer of shares in a related foreign company to minor children without the other shareholder's knowledge.
key_quote: The unlawful transfer of shares without the knowledge or consent of the other shareholder may constitute oppressive conduct.
full_summary: Two brothers who were sole shareholders and directors of Claddagh Jewellers brought cross-claims of oppression under s.212 of the Companies Act 2014. The allegations included oppressive conduct, breach of a settlement agreement, and the unlawful transfer of shares in a related Spanish company to minor children without the knowledge of the other shareholder. The case illustrates the intersection of s.212 oppression proceedings with breach of settlement agreements and international asset disputes.
